sp.l: GRamercie Letherleg: Get me the Spurrier,His Shooemaker has pull'd on a new payre of bootes; and hee walks in his Gowne, waſtcoate, and trouſes, expecting his Taylor.
And thou haſt fitted me.

sp.speaker: LET.
sp.l: I'll do't preſently.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Look to me, wit, and look to my wit, Land,
That is, looke on me, and with all thine eyes,
Male, Female, yea, Hermaphroditicke eyes,
And thoſe bring all your helpes, and perſpicills,
To ſee me at beſt aduantage, and augment
My forme as I come forth, for I doe feele
I will be one, worth looking after, ſhortly.
Now, by and by, that's ſhortly.He drawes foorth his watch, and ſets it on the Table. 't ſtrikes! One, two,
Three, foure, fiue, ſix. Inough, inough, deare watch,
Thy pulſe hath beate inough. Now ſleepe, and reſt;
Would thou couldſt make the time to doe ſo too:
I'll winde thee vp no more. The houre is come
So long expected! There, there,He throws off his gowne drop my wardſhip,
My pupill age, and vaſſalage together.
And Liberty, come throw thy ſelfe about me,
In a rich ſuite, cloake, hat, and band, for now
I'le ſue out no mans Liuery, but miny owne,
I ſtand on my owne feete, ſo much a yeere,
Right, round, and ſound, the Lord of mine owne ground,
And (to ryme to it) threeſcore thouſand Pound!
He goes to the doore, and lookes. Not come? Not yet? Taylor thou art a vermine,
Worſe then the ſame thou proſecut'ſt, and prick'ſt
In ſubtill ſeame— (Go too, I ſay no more)
Thus to retard my longings: on the day
I doe write man, to beat thee. One and twenty,
Since the clock ſtrooke, compleat! and thou wilt feele it
Thou fooliſh Animall! I could pitty him,
(An' I were not heartily angry with him now)
For this one peece of folly he beares about him,
To dare to tempt the Furie of an heyre,
T' aboue two thouſand a yeere; yet hope his cuſtome!
Well, Mr. Faſhioner, theres ſome muſt breake—
A head, for this your breaking. Are you come, Sir,
GOd giue your worſhip ioy.

sp.speaker: P.IV.
sp.l: What? of your ſtaying?
And leauing me to ſtalke here in my trowſes,
Like a tame Her'n-few for you?

sp.speaker: FAS.
sp.l: I but waited
Below, till the clocke ſtrooke.

sp.speaker: P.IV.
sp.l: Why, if you had come
Before a quarter, would it ſo haue hurt you,
In reputation, to haue wayted here?

sp.speaker: FAS.
sp.l: No, but your worſhip might haue pleaded nonage,
If you had got 'hem on, ere I could make
Iuſt Affidauit of the time.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: That ieſt
Has gain'd thy pardon, thou had'ſt liu'd, condemn'd
To thine owne hell elſe, neuer to haue wrought
Stitch more for me, or any Peniboy,
I could haue hindred thee: but now thou art mine.
For one and twenty yeeres, or for three liues,
Chuſe which thou wilt, I'll make thee a Copy-holder,
He ſayes his ſute. And thy firſt Bill vnqueſtion'd. Helpe me on.

sp.speaker: FAS.
sp.l: Preſently, Sir, I am bound vnto your worſhip.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Thou ſhalt be, when I haue ſeal'd thee a Leaſe of my Cuſtome.

sp.speaker: FAS.
sp.l: Your worps. Barbar is without.

sp.speaker: P.IN.
sp.l: Who? Thom?
Come in Thom: ſet thy things vpon the Boord
And ſpread thy clothes, lay all forth in procinctu,
And tell's what newes?

sp.speaker: THO.
sp.l: O Sir, a ſtaple of newes!
Or the New Staple, which you pleaſe.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: What's that?

sp.speaker: FAS.
sp.l: An Office, Sir, a braue young Office ſet vp.
I had forgot to tell your worſhip.

sp.speaker: P.IV.
sp.l: For what?

sp.speaker: THO.
sp.l: To enter all the Newes, Sir, o' the time,

sp.speaker: FAS.
sp.l: And vent it as occaſion ſerues! A place
Of huge commerce it will be!

sp.speaker: P.IV.
sp.l: Pray thee peace,
I cannot abide a talking Taylor: let Thom
(He's a Barber) by his place relate it,
What is't, an Office, Thom?

sp.speaker: THO.
sp.l: Newly erected
Here in the houſe, almoſt on the ſame floore,
Where all the newes of all ſorts ſhall be brought,
And there be examin'd, and then regiſtred,
And ſo be iſſu'd vnder the Seale of the Office,
As Staple Newes; no other newes be currant.

sp.speaker: P.IV.
sp.l: 'Fore me, thou ſpeak'ſt of a braue buſines, Thom.

sp.speaker: FAS.
sp.l: Nay, if you knew the brain that hatch'd it Sr—

sp.speaker: P.IV.
sp.l: I know thee wel inough: giue him a loaf, Thom—
Quiet his mouth, that Ouen will be venting elſe.
Proceed—

sp.speaker: THO.
sp.l: He tels you true Sr. Mr Cymbal,
Is Maſter of the Office, he proiected it,
Hee lies here i'the houſe: and the great roomes
He has taken for the Office, and ſet vp
His Deskes and Claſſes, Tables and his Shelues,

sp.speaker: FAS.
sp.l: He's my Cuſtomer, and a Wit Sir, too.
But, h'has braue wits vnder him—

sp.speaker: THO.
sp.l: Yes, foure Emiſſaries,

sp.speaker: P.IV.
sp.l: Emiſſaries? ſtay, there's a fine new word, Thom!
'Pray God it ſignifie any thing, what are Emiſſaries?

sp.speaker: THO.
sp.l: Men imploy'd outward, that are ſent abroad
To fetch in the commodity.

sp.speaker: FAS.
sp.l: From all regions
Where the beſt newes are made.

sp.speaker: THO.
sp.l: Or vented forth.

sp.speaker: FAS.
sp.l: By way of exchange, or trade.

sp.speaker: P.IV.
sp.l: Nay, thou wilt ſpeak—

sp.speaker: FAS.
sp.l: My ſhare Sr. there's enough for both.

sp.speaker: P.IV.
sp.l: Goe on then, Hee giues the Taylor leaue to talk.
Speake all thou canſt: me thinkes, the ordinaries
Should helpe them much.

sp.speaker: FAS.
sp.l: Sir, they haue ordinaries,
And extraordinaries, as many changes,
And variations, as there are points i'the compaſſe.

sp.speaker: THO.
sp.l: But the 4. Cardinall Quarters—

sp.speaker: P.IV.
sp.l: I, thoſe Thom—

sp.speaker: THO.
sp.l: The Court, Sir, Pauls, Exchange, and Weſtminſter-hall.

sp.speaker: P.IV.
sp.l: Who is the Chiefe? which hath preceedencie?

sp.speaker: THO.
sp.l: The gouernour o'the Staple, Maſter Cymball.
He is the Chiefe; and after him the Emiſſaries:
Firſt Emiſſary Court, one Maſter Fitton,
He's a Ieerer too.

sp.speaker: P.IV.
sp.l: What's that?

sp.speaker: FAS.
sp.l: A Wit.

sp.speaker: THO.
sp.l: Or halfe a Wit, ſome of them are Halfe-wits,
Two to a Wit, there are a ſet of 'hem.
Then Maſter Ambler, Emiſſary Paules,
A fine pac'd gentleman, as you ſhall ſee, walke
The middle Ile: And then my Froy Hans Buz,
A Dutch-man; he's Emiſſary Exhange.

sp.speaker: FAS.
sp.l: I had thought Mr. Burſt the Marchant had had it.

sp.speaker: THO.
sp.l: No,
He has a rupture, hee has ſprung a leake,
Emiſſarie Weſtminſter's vndiſpos'd of yet;
Then the Examiner, Regiſter, and two Clerkes,
They mannage all at home, and ſort, and file,
And ſeale the newes, and iſſue them.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Thom, deare Thom.
What may my means doe for thee, aske, and haue it,
I'd faine be doing ſome good. It is my birth-day.
And I'd doe it betimes, I feele a grudging
Of bounty, and I would not long lye fallow.
I pray thee thinke, and ſpeake, or wiſh for ſomething.

sp.speaker: THO.
sp.l: I would I had but one o' the Clerkes places,
I'this Newes Office,.

sp.speaker: P.IV.
sp.l: Thou ſhalt haue it, Thom,
If ſiluer, or gold will fetch it; what's the rate?
At what is't ſet i'the Mercat?

sp.speaker: THO.
sp.l: Fiftie pound, Sir.

sp.speaker: P.IV.
sp.l: An't were a hundred, Thom, thou ſhalt not want it.

sp.speaker: FAS.
sp.l: The Taylor leapes, and embraceth him. O Noble Maſter!

sp.speaker: P.IV.
sp.l: How now Aeſops Aſſe!
Becauſe I play with Thom, muſt I needes runne
Into your rude embraces? ſtand you ſtill, Sir;
Clownes fawnings, are a horſes ſalutations.
How do'ſt thou like my ſuite, Thom?

sp.speaker: THO.
sp.l: Mr Faſhioner
Has hit your meaſures, Sir, h'has moulded you,
And made you, as they ſay.

sp.speaker: FAS.
sp.l: No, no, not I,
I am an Aſſe, old Aeſops Aſſe.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Nay, Faſhioner,
I can doe thee a good turne too, be not muſty,
Though thou haſt moulded me, as little Thom ſayes,
He drawes out his pockets.(I thinke thou haſt put me in mouldy pockets.)

sp.speaker: FAS.
sp.l: As good,
Right Spaniſh perfume, the Lady Eſtifania's,
They coſt twelue pound a payre.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Thy bill will ſay ſo.
I pray thee tell me, Faſhioner, what Authors
Thou read'ſt to helpe thy inuention? Italian prints?
Or Arras hangings? They are Taylors Libraries.

sp.speaker: FAS.
sp.l: I ſcorne ſuch helps.

sp.speaker: P.IV.
sp.l: O, though thou art a ſilk-worme!
And deal'ſt in ſattins and veluets, and rich pluſhes,
Thou canſt not ſpin all formes out of thy ſelfe;
They are quite other things: I thinke this ſuite
Has made me wittier, then I was.

sp.speaker: FAS.
sp.l: Belieue it Sir,
That clothes doe much vpon the wit, as weather
Do's on the braine; and thence comes your prouerbe;
The Taylor makes the man: I ſpeake by experience
Of my owne Cuſtomers. I haue had Gallants,
Both Court and Countrey, would ha' fool'd you vp
In a new ſuite, with the beſt wits, in being,
And kept their ſpeed, as long as their clothes laſted
Han'ſome, and neate; but then as they grew out
At the elbowes againe, or had a ſtaine, or ſpot,
They haue ſunke moſt wretchedly.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: What thou reportſt,
Is but the common calamity, and ſeene daily;
And therefore you 'haue another anſwering prouerbe:

l.hi: A broken ſleeue keepes the arme backe,

sp.speaker: FAS.
sp.l: 'Tis true, Sir.
And thence wee ſay, that ſuch a one playes at peepe-arme.

sp.speaker: P.IV.
sp.l: Doe you ſo? it is wittily ſayd. I wonder, Gentlemen,
And men of meanes will not maintaine themſelues
Freſher in wit, I meane in clothes, to the higheſt.
For hee that's out o' clothes, is out o'faſhion,
And out of faſhion, is out of countenance,
And out o' countenance, is out o' Wit.
Is not Rogue Haberdaſher come?

sp.speaker: HAB.
sp.l: Yes, here, Sir. They are all about him, buſie.
I ha' beene without this halfe houre.

sp.speaker: P.IV.
sp.l: Giue me my hat.
Put on my Girdle. Raſcall, ſits my Ruffe well?

sp.speaker: LIN.
sp.l: In print.

sp.speaker: P.IV.
sp.l: Slaue.

sp.speaker: LIN.
sp.l: See your ſelfe.

sp.speaker: P.IV.
sp.l: Is this ſame hat
O'the blocke paſſant? Doe not anſwer mee,
I cannot ſtay for an anſwer. I doe feele
The powers of one and twenty, like a Tide
Flow in vpon mee, and perceiue an Heyre,
Can Coniure vp all ſpirits in all circles,
Rogue, Raſcall, Slaue, giue tradeſmen their true names,
And they appeare to 'hem preſently.

sp.speaker: LIN.
sp.l: For profit.

sp.speaker: P.IV.
sp.l: Come, caſt my cloake about me, I'll goe ſee,
This Office Thom, and be trimm'd afterwards.
I'll put thee in poſſeſſion, my prime worke!
Gods ſo: my Spurrier! put 'hem on boy, quickly, His Spurrier comes in.
I'had like to ha loſt my Spurres with too much ſpeed.
Good morning to my Ioy, My iolly Peni-boy!

l.hi: The Lord, and the Prince of plenty!
I come to ſee what riches, Thou beareſt in thy breeches,
The firſt of thy one and twenty:
What, doe thy pockets gingle? Or ſhall wee neede to mingle
Our ſtrength both of foote, and horſes!
Theſe fellows looke ſo eager, As if they would beleaguer
An Heyre in the midſt of his forces!

sp.l: I hope they be no Serieants! That hang vpon thy margents.
This Rogue has the Ioule of a Iaylor!

sp.speaker: P.IV.
sp.l:
O Founder, no ſuch matter, My Spurrier, and my Hatter, The young Peny-bo •
anſw •
My Linnen man, and my Taylor.
Thou ſhould'ſt haue beene brought in too, Shoomaker,
If the time had beene longer, and Thom Barber.
How do'ſt thou like my company, old Canter?
Doe I not muſter a braue troupe? all Bill-men?
Preſent your Armes, before my Founder here,
This is my Founder, this ſame learned Canter!
He brought me the firſt newes of my fathers death,
He tales the bils, and puts them vp in his pockets. I thanke him, and euer ſince, I call him Founder,
Worſhip him, boyes, I'll read onely the ſummes.
And paſſe 'hem ſtreight.

sp.speaker: SHO.
sp.l: Now Ale.

sp.speaker: REST.
sp.l: And ſtrong Ale bleſſe him.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Gods ſo, ſome Ale, and Sugar for my Founder!
Good Bills, ſufficient Bills, theſe Bills may paſſe.

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: I do not like thoſe paper-ſquibs, good Maſter.
They may vndoe your ſtore, I meane, of Credit,
And fire your Arſenall, if caſe you doe not
In time make good thoſe outerworkes, your pockets,
And take a Garriſon in of ſome two hundred,
To beat theſe Pyoners off, that carry a Mine
Would blow you vp, at laſt. Secure your Caſamates,
Here Maſter Picklocke, Sir, your man o' Law,
And learn'd Atturney, has ſent you a Bag of munition.

sp.speaker: P.IV.
sp.l: What is't?

sp.speaker: P.CA.
sp.l: Three hundred pieces.

sp.speaker: P.IV.
sp.l: I'll diſpatch 'hem.

sp.speaker: P.CA.
sp.l: Do, I would haue your ſtrengths lin'd, and perfum'd
With Gold, as well as Amber.

sp.speaker: P.IV.
sp.l: God a mercy,
Come, Ad ſoluendum, boyes! there, there, and there, &c.
He payes all. I looke on nothing but Totalis.

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: See!
The difference 'twixt the couetous, and the prodigall!
"The Couetous man neuer has money! and
"The Prodigall will haue none ſhortly!

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Ha,
What ſaies my Founder? I thanke you, I thanke you Sirs.

sp.speaker: ALL.
sp.l: God bleſſe your worſhip, and your worſhips Chanter.

sp.speaker: P CA.
sp.l: I ſay 't is nobly done, to cheriſh Shop-keepers,
And pay their Bills, without examining thus.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Alas! they haue had a pittifull hard time on't,
A long vacation, from their coozening.
Poore Raſcalls, I doe doe it out of charity.
I would aduance their trade againe, and haue them
Haſte to be rich, ſweare, and forſweare wealthily,
What doe you ſtay for, Sirrah?

sp.speaker: SPV.
sp.l: To my boxe Sir,

sp.speaker: P.IV.
sp.l: Your boxe, why, there's an angel, if my Spurres
He giues the Spurrier, to his boxe. Be not right Rippon.

sp.speaker: SPV.
sp.l: Giue me neuer a penny
If I ſtrike not thorow your bounty with the Rowells.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Do'ſt thou want any money Founder?

sp.speaker: P.CA.
sp.l: Who, Sr. I,
Did I not tell you I was bred i'the Mines,
Vnder Sir Beuis Bullion.

sp.speaker: P.IV.
sp.l: That is true,
I quite forgot, you Myne-men want no money,
Your ſtreets are pau'd with 't: there, the molten ſiluer
Runns out like creame, on cakes of gold.

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: And Rubies
Doe grow like Strawberries.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: 'Twere braue being there!
Come Thom, we'll go to the Office now.

sp.speaker: P.CA.
sp.l: What Office?

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Newes Office, the New Staple; thou ſhalt goe too,
'Tis here i'the houſe, on the ſame floore, Thom. ſayes,
Come, Founder, let vs trade in Ale, and nutmegges.
WHat, are thoſe Desks fit now? ſet forth the Table,
The Carpet and the Chayre: where are the Newes
That were examin'd laſt? ha' you fil'd them vp?

sp.speaker: CLE.
sp.l: Not yet, I had no time.

sp.speaker: REG.
sp.l: Are thoſe newes regiſtred,
That Emiſſary Buz ſent in laſt night?
Of Spinola, and his Egges?

sp.speaker: CLE.
sp.l: Yes Sir, and fil'd.

sp.speaker: REG.
sp.l: What are you now vpon?

sp.speaker: CLE.
sp.l: That our new Emiſſary
Weſtminſter, gaue vs, of the Golden Heyre.

sp.speaker: REG.
sp.l: Diſpatch, that's newes indeed, and of importance.
What would you haue good woman?

sp.speaker: WO.
sp.l: I would haue Sir, A countrey-woman waites ther •
A groatſworth of any Newes, I care not what,
To carry downe this Saturday, to our Vicar.

sp.speaker: REG.
sp.l: O! You are a Butterwoman, aske Nathaniel
The Clerke, there.

sp.speaker: CLE.
sp.l: Sir, I tell her, ſhe muſt ſtay
Till Emiſſary Exchange, or Pauls ſend in,
And then I'll fit her.

sp.speaker: REG.
sp.l: Doe good woman, haue patience,
It is not now, as when the Captaine liu'd.

sp.speaker: CLE.
sp.l: You'll blaſt the reputation of the Office,
Now i'the Bud, if you diſpatch theſe Groats,
So ſoone: let them attend in name of policie.

div.head: ACT. I.
SCENE. V.
PENIBOY. CYMBAL. FITTON. THO: BARBER. CANTER.

sp.l: IN troth they are dainty roomes; what place is this?

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: This is the outer roome, where my Clerkes ſit,
And keepe their ſides, the Regiſter i'the midſt,
The Examiner, he ſits priuate there, within,
And here I haue my ſeuerall Rowles, and Fyles
Of Newes by the Alphabet, and all put vp
Vnder their heads.

sp.speaker: P.IV.
sp.l: But thoſe, too, ſubdiuided?

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: Into Authenticall, and Apocryphall.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Or Newes of doubtfull credit, as Barbers newes.

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: And Taylors Newes, Porters, and Watermens newes,

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Whereto, beſide the Coranti, and Gazetti.

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: I haue the Newes of the ſeaſon.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: As vacation newes,
Terme-nerves, and Chriſtmas-newes.

sp.speaker: CIM.
sp.l: And newes O' the faction.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: As the Reformed newes, Proteſtant newes,

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: And Pontificiall newes, of all which ſeuerall,
The Day-bookes, Characters, Precedents are kept.
Together with the names of ſpeciall friends—

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: And men of Correſpondence i'the Countrey—

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: Yes, of all ranks, and all Religions.—

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Factors, and Agents—

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: Liegers, that lie out
Through all the Shires o'the kingdome.

sp.speaker: P.IV.
sp.l: This is fine!
And beares a braue relation! but what ſayes
Mercurius Britannicus to this?

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: O Sir, he gaines by't halfe in halfe.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Nay more
I'll ſtand to't. For, where he was wont to get
In, hungry Captaines, obſcure Stateſmen.

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: Fellowes
To drinke with him in a darke roome in a Tauerne,
And eat a Sawſage.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: We ha' ſeen't,

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: As faine,
To keepe ſo many politique pennes
Going, to feed the preſſe.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: And diſh our newes,
Were't true, or falſe.

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: Now all that charge is ſau'd
The publique Chronicler.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: How, doe you call him there?

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: And gentle Reader.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: He that has the maidenhead
Of all the bookes.

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: Yes, dedicated to him,

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Or rather proſtituted.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: You are right, Sir.

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: No more ſhall be abus'd, nor countrey-Parſons
O' the Inquiſition, nor buſie Iuſtices,
Trouble the peace, and both torment themſelues,
And their poore ign'rant Neighbours with enquiries
After the many, and moſt innocent Monſters,
That neuer came i'th' Counties they were charg'd with.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Why, me thinkes Sir, if the honeſt common people
Will be abus'd, why ſhould not they ha' their pleaſure,
In the belieuing Lyes, are made for them;
As you i'th' Office, making them your ſelues?

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: O Sir! it is the printing we oppoſe.

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: We not forbid that any Newes, be made,
But that 't be printed; for when Newes is printed,
It leaues Sir to be Newes. while 'tis but written —

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Though it be ne're ſo falſe, it runnes Newes ſtill.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: See diuers mens opinions! vnto ſome,
The very printing of them, makes them Newes;
That ha' not the heart to beleeue any thing,
But what they ſee in print.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: I, that's an Error
Ha's abus'd many; but we ſhall reforme it,
As many things beſide (we haue a hope)
Are crept among the popular abuſes.

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: Nor ſhall the Stationer cheat vpon the Time,
By buttering ouer againe—

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: once, in Seuen Yeares,
As the age doates—

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: And growes forgetfull o'them,
His antiquated Pamphlets, with new dates.
But all ſhall come from the Mint.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Freſh and new ſtamp'd,

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: With the Office-Seale, Staple Commoditie.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: And if a man will aſſure his Newes, he may:
Two-pence a Sheet he ſhall be warranted,
And haue a policie for't.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Sir, I admire
The method o' your place; all things within't
Are ſo digeſted, fitted, and compos'd,
As it ſhewes Wit had married Order.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Sir.

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: The beſt wee could to inuite the Times.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: It ha's
Coſt ſweat, and freeſing.

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: And ſome broken ſleepes
Before it came to this.

sp.speaker: P.IV.
sp.l: I eaſily thinke it.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: But now it ha's the ſhape—

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: And is come forth.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: A moſt polite neat thing! with all the limbs,
As ſenſe can taſt!

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: It is Sir, though I ſay it,
As well-begotten a buſines, and as fairely
Helpt to the World.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: You muſt be a Mid-wife Sir!
Or els the ſonne of a Mid-wife! (pray you pardon me)
Haue helpt it forth ſo happily! what Newes ha' you?
Newes o' this morning? I would faine heare ſome
Freſh, from the forge (as new as day, as they ſay.)

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: And ſuch we haue Sir.

sp.speaker: REG.
sp.l: Shew him the laſt Rowle,
Of Emiſſary Weſt-minſter's, The Heire.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Come nearer, Thom:

sp.speaker: CLA.
sp.l: There is a braue yong Heire
Peny reioyceth, that he i • in. Is come of age this morning, Mr. Peny-boy.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: That's I!

sp.speaker: CLA.
sp.l: His Father dy'd on this day ſeuenth-night.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: True!

sp.speaker: CLA.
sp.l: At ſixe o'the Clocke i'the morning, iuſt a weeke
Tels Thom: of it. Ere he was One and Twenty.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: I am here, Thom!
Proceed, I pray thee.

sp.speaker: CLA.
sp.l: An old Canting Begger
Brought him firſt Newes, whom he has entertain'd,
Call in the Canter. Hee giues the Clerke. To follow him, ſince.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Why, you ſhall ſee him! Founder,
Come in; no Follower, but Companion,
I pray thee put him in, Friend. There's an Angell—
Thou do'ſt not know, hee's a wiſe old Fellow,
Though he ſeeme patch'd thus, and made vp o' peeces.
Founder, we are in, here, in, i'the Newes-Office!
In this dayes Rowle, already! I doe muſe
How you came by vs Sir's!

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: One Maſter Pick-locke
A Lawyer, that hath purchas'd here a place,
This morning, of an Emiſſary vnder me.

l.hi: Emiſſarie Weſtminſter.

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: Gaue it into th'Office,

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: For his Eſſay, his peece.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: My man o' Law!
Hee's my Attorney, and Sollicitour too!
A fine pragmaticke! what's his place worth?

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: A Nemo-ſcit, Sir.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: 'Tis as Newes come, in,

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: And as they are iſſued. I haue the iuſt meoytie
For my part: then the other moeytie
Is parted into ſeuen. The foure Emiſſaries;
Whereof my Cozen Fitton here's for Court,
Ambler for Pauls, and Buz for the Exchange,
Picklocke, for Weſtminſter, with the Examiner,
And Regiſter, they haue full parts: and then one part
Is vnder-parted to a couple of Clarkes;
And there's the iuſt diuiſion of the profits!

sp.speaker: P.IV.
sp.l: Ha' you thoſe Clarks Sir.

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: There is one Desk empty,
But it has many Suitors.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Sir, may I
Preſent one more and carry it, if his parts
Or Gifts, (which you will, call'hem)

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: Be ſufficient Sir.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: What are your preſent Clarkes habilities?
How is he qualified?

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: A decay'd Stationer
He was, but knowes Newes well, can ſort and ranke 'hem.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: And for a need can make 'hem.

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: True Paules bred,
I'the Church-yard.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: And this at the Weſt-dore,
O'th other ſide, hee's my Barber Thom,
A pretty Scholler, and a Maſter of Arts,
Was made, or went out Maſter of Arts in a throng,
At the Vniuerſitie; as before, one Chriſtmas,
He got into a Maſque at Court, by his wit,
And the good meanes of his Cythern, holding vp thus
For one o'the Muſique, Hee's a nimble Fellow▪
And alike skil'd in euery liberall Science,
As hauing certaine ſnaps of all, a neat,
Quick-vaine, in forging Newes too. I doe loue him,
And promis'd him a good turne, and I would doe it.
Whats your price? the value?

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: Fifty pounds, Sr.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Get in Thom, take poſſeſſion, I inſtall thee;

Here, tell your money; giue thee ioy, good Thom;
Hee buyes Thom a Clerkes place.
And let me heare from thee euery minute of Newes,
While the New Staple ſtands, or the Office laſts,
Which I doe wiſh, may ne're be leſſe for thy ſake.

sp.speaker: CLA.
sp.l: The Emiſſaries, Sir, would ſpeake with you,
And Maſter Fitton, they haue brought in Newes,
Three Bale together.

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: Sr, you are welcome, here. They take leaue of Peny-boy, and Canter.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: So is your creature.

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: Buſineſſe calls vs off, Sir,
That may concerne the Office.

sp.speaker: P.IV.
sp.l: Keepe me faire, Sir,
Still i'your Staple, I am here your friend,
On the ſame flooer.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: We ſhall be your ſeruants.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: How doſt thou like it, Founder?

sp.speaker: P.CA.
sp.l: All is well,
But that your man o' law me thinks appeares not
In his due time. O! Here comes Maſters worſhip.
HOw do's the Heyre, bright Maſter Peniboy?
Is hee awake yet in his One and Twenty?
Why, this is better farre, then to weare Cypreſſe,
Dull ſmutting gloues, or melancholy blacks,
And haue a payre of twelue-peny broad ribbands
Laid out like Labells.

sp.speaker: P.IV.
sp.l: I ſhould ha' made ſhift
To haue laught as heartily in my mourners hood,
As in this Suite, if it had pleas'd my father
To haue beene buried, with the Trumpeters.

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: The Heralds of Armes, you meane.

sp.speaker: P.IV.
sp.l: I meane,
All noyſe, that is ſuperfluous!

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: All that idle pompe,
And vanity of a Tombe-ſtone, your wiſe father
Did, by his will, preuent. Your worſhip had—

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: A louing and obedient father of him,
I know it: a right, kinde-natur'd man,
To dye ſo opportunely.

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: And to ſettle
All things ſo well, compounded for your ward ſhip
The weeke afore, and left your ſtate entyre
Without any charge vpon't.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: I muſt needes ſay,
I loſt an Officer of him, a good Bayliffe,
And I ſhall want him; but all peace be with him,
I will not wiſh him aliue, againe; not I,
For all my Fortune; giue your worſhip ioy
O'your new place, your Emiſſary-ſhip,
I'the Newes Office.

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: Know you, why I bought it Sr?

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Not I.

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: To worke for you, and carry a myne
Againſt the Maſter of it, Maſter Cymball;
Who hath a plot vpon a Gentlewoman,
Was once deſign'd for you, Sir.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Me?

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: Your father,
Old Maſter Peni-boy, of happy memory,
And wiſdome too, as any i'the County,
Carefull to finde out a fit match for you,
In his owne life time (but hee was preuented)
Left it in writing in a Schedule here,
To be annexed to his Will; that you,
His onely Sonne, vpon his charge, and bleſſing,
Should take due notice of a Gentlewoman,
Soiourning with your vncle, Rieher Peni-boy.

sp.speaker: P.IV.
sp.l: A Corniſh Gentlewoman, I doe know her,
Miſtreſſe, Pecunia doe-all.

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: A great Lady,
Indeede ſhee is, and not of mortall race,
Infanta of the Mines; her Graces Grandfather,
Was Duke, and Couſin to the King of Ophyr,
The Subterranean, let that paſſe. Her name is,
Or rather, her three names are (for ſuch ſhee is)
Aurelia Clara Pecunia, A great Princeſſe,
Of mighty power, though ſhee liue in priuate
With a contracted family! Her Secretary—

sp.speaker: P.CA.
sp.l: Who is her Gentleman-vſher too.

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: One Broker,
And then two Gentlewomen; Miſtreſſe Statute,
And Miſtreſſe Band, with Waxe the Chambermaide,
And Mother Mortgage, the old Nurſe, two Groomes,
Pawne, and his fellow; you haue not many to bribe, Sir.
The worke is feizible, and th'approches eaſie,
By your owne kindred. Now, Sir, Cymball thinkes,
The Maſter here, and gouernor o'the Staple,
By his fine arts, and pompe of his great place
To draw her! He concludes, ſhee is a woman!
And that ſo ſoone as ſh' heares of the New Office,
Shee'll come to viſit it, as they all haue longings
After new ſights, and motions! But your bounty,
Perſon, and brauery muſt atchieue her.

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: Shee is
The talke o'the time! th'aduenture o'the age!

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: You cannot put your ſelfe vpon an action
Of more importance.

sp.speaker: P.CA.
sp.l: All the world are ſuiters to her.

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: All ſorts of men, and all profeſſions!

sp.speaker: P.CA.
sp.l: You ſhall haue ſtall-fed Doctors, cram'd Diuines
Make loue to her, and with thoſe ſtudied
And perfum'd flatteries, as no rome can ſtinke
More elegant, then where they are.

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: VVell chanted
Old Canter thou ſingſt true.

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: And (by your leaue)
Good Maſters worſhip, ſome of your veluet coate
Make corpulent curt'ſies to her, till they cracke for't.

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: There's Doctor Almanack wooes her, one of the Ieerers,
A fine Phyſitian.

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: Your Sea-captaine, Shun-field,
Giues out hee'll goe vpon the Cannon for her.

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: Though his lowd mouthing get him little credit,

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: Young Maſter Pyed-mantle, the fine Herrald
Profeſſes to deriuer her through all ages,
From all the Kings, and Queenes, that euer were.

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: And Maſter Madrigall, the crowned Poet
Of theſe our times, doth offer at her praiſes
As faire as any, when it ſhall pleaſe Apollo,
That wit and rime may meete both in one ſubiect.

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: And you to beare her from all theſe, it will be—

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: A work of fame.

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: Of honor.

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: Celebration.

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: Worthy your name.

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: The Peni-boyes to liue in't,

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: It is an action you were built for, Sir,

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: And none but you can doe it.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: I'll vndertake it,

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: And carry it.

sp.speaker: P.IV.
sp.l: Feare me not, for ſince I came
Of mature age, I haue had a certaine itch
In my right eye, this corner, here, doe you ſee?
To doe ſome worke, and worthy of a Chronicle.

div.head: The firſt Intermeane after the firſt Act.

sp.speaker: MIRTH.
sp.p: How now Goſsip! how doe's the Play pleaſe you?

sp.speaker: CENSVRE.
sp.p: Very ſcuruily, me thinks, and ſufficiently naught.

sp.speaker: EXPECTATION.
sp.p: As a body would wiſh: here's nothing but a young Prodigall, come of age, who makes much of the Barber, buyes him a place in a new Office, i'the ayre, I know not where, and his man o' Law to follow him, with the Begger to boote, and they two helpe him to a wiſe.

sp.speaker: MIRTH.
sp.p: I, ſhee is a proper piece! that ſuch creatures can broke for.

sp.speaker: TATLE.
sp.p: I cannot abide that naſty fellow, the Begger, if hee had beene a Court-Begger in good clothes; a Begger in veluet, as they ſay, I could haue endur'd him.

sp.speaker: MIRTH.
sp.p: Or a begging ſcholler in blacke, or one of theſe beggerly Poets, goſsip, that would hang vpon a young heyre like a horſeleech.

sp.speaker: EXPEC.
sp.p: Or a thred-bare Doctor of Phyſicke, a poore Quackeſaluer.

sp.speaker: CENSVRE.
sp.p: Or a Sea-captaine, halfe ſteru'd.

sp.speaker: MIRTH.
sp.p: I, theſe were tolerable Beggers, Beggers of faſhion! you ſhall ſee ſome ſuch anon!

sp.speaker: TATLE.
sp.p: I would faine ſee the Foole, goſsip, the Foole is the fineſt man i'the company, they ſay, and has all the wit: Hee is the very Iuſtice o' Peace o' the Play, and can cemmit whom hee will, and what hee will, errour, abſurdity, as the toy takes him, and no man ſay, blacke is his eye, but laugh at him.

sp.speaker: MIRTH.
sp.p: But they ha' no Foole i' this Play, I am afraid, goſsip.

sp.speaker: TATLE.
sp.p: It's a wiſe Play, then.

sp.speaker: EXPECTATION.
sp.p: They are all fooles, the rather, in that.

sp.speaker: CENSVRE.
sp.p: Like enough.

sp.speaker: TATLE.
sp.p: My husband, (Timothy Tatle, God reſt his poore ſoule) was wont to ſay, there was no Play without a Foole, and a Diuell in't; he was for the Diuell ſtill, God bleſſe him. The Diuell for his money, would hee ſay, I would faine ſee the Diuell. And why would you ſo faine ſee the Diuell? would I ſay. Becauſe hee has hornes, wife, and may be a cuckold, as well as a Diuell, hee would anſwer: You are e'en ſuch another, husband, quoth I. Was the Diuell euer married? where doe you read, the Diuell was euer ſo honorable to commit Matrimony; The Play will tell vs, that, ſayes hee, wee'll goe ſee't to morrow, the Diuell is an Aſſe. Hee is an errant learn'd man, that made it, and can write, they ſay, and I am fouly deceiu'd, but hee can read too.

sp.speaker: MIRTH.
sp.p: I remember it goſsip, I went with you, by the ſame token, Mrs. Trouble Truth diſwaded vs, and told vs, hee was a prophane Poet, and all his Playes had Diuels in them. That he kept ſchole vpo' the Stage, could coniure there, aboue the Schole of Weſtminſter, and Doctor Lamb too: not a Play he made, but had a Diuell in it. And that he would learne vs all to make our husbands Cuckolds at Playes: by another token, that a young married wife i'the company, ſaid, ſhee could finde in her heart to ſteale thither, and ſee a little o'the vanity through her maſque, and come practice at home.

sp.speaker: TATLE.
sp.p: O, it was, Miſtreſſe—

sp.speaker: MIRTH.
sp.p: Nay, Goſsip, I name no body. It may be 'twas my ſelfe.

sp.speaker: EXPECTATION.
sp.p: But was the Diuell a proper man, Goſsip?

sp.speaker: MIRTH.
sp.p: As fine a gentleman, of his inches, as euer I ſaw truſted to the Stage, or any where elſe: and lou'd the common wealth, well as ere a Patriot of 'hem all: hee would carry away the Vice on his backe, quicke to Hell, in euery Play where he came, and reforme abuſes.

sp.speaker: EXPECTATION.
sp.p: There was the Diuell of Edmonton, no ſuch man, I warrant you.

sp.speaker: CENSVRE.
sp.p: The Coniurer cooſen'd him with a candles end, hee was an Aſſe.

sp.speaker: MIRTH.
sp.p: But there was one Smug, a Smith, would haue made a horſe laugh, and broke his halter, as they ſay.

sp.speaker: TATLE.
sp.p: O, but the poore man had got a ſhrewd miſchance, one day.

sp.speaker: EXPECTATION.
sp.p: How, Goſsip?

sp.speaker: TATLE.
sp.p: He had dreſt a Rogue Iade i' the morning, that had the Staggers, and had got ſuch a ſpice of 'hem himſelfe, by noone, as they would not away all the Play time, doe what hee could, for his heart.

sp.speaker: MIRTH.
sp.p: 'Twas his part, Goſsip, he was to be drunke, by his part.

sp.speaker: TATLE.
sp.p: Say you ſo, I vnderſtood not ſo much.

sp.speaker: EXPECTA.
sp.p: Would wee had ſuch an other part, and ſuch a man in this play, I feare 'twill be an excellent dull thing.

sp.speaker: CENSVRE.
sp.p: Expect, intend it.

div.head: ACT. II.
SCENE. I.
PENI-BOY. Sen. PECVNIA. MORTGAGE. STATVTE. BAND. BROKER.

sp.l: YOur Grace is ſad me thinks, and melancholy!
You doe not looke vpon me with that face,
As you were wont, my Goddeſſe, bright Pecunia:
Although your Grace be falne, of two i'the hundred,
In vulgar eſtimation; yet am I,
You Graces ſeruant ſtill: and teach this body,
To bend, and theſe my aged knees to buckle,
In adoration, and iuſt worſhip of you.
Indeed, I doe confeſſe, I haue no ſhape
To make a minion of, but I'm your Martyr,
Your Graces Martyr. I can heare the Rogues,
As I doe walke the ſtreetes, whiſper, and point,
There goes old Peni-boy, the ſlaue of money,
Rich Peni-boy, Lady Pecunia's drudge,
A ſordid Raſcall, one that neuer made
Good meale in his ſleep, but ſells the acates are ſent him,
Fiſh, Fowle, and veniſon, and preſerues himſelfe,
Like an old hoary Rat, with mouldy pye-cruſt.
This I doe heare, reioycing, I can ſuffer
This, and much more, for your good Graces ſake.

sp.speaker: PEC.
sp.l: Why do you ſo my Guardian? I not bid you,
Cannot my Grace be gotten, and held too,
Without your ſelfe-tormentings, and your watches,
Your macerating of your body thus
With cares, and ſcantings of your dyet, and reſt?

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: O, no, your ſeruices, my Princely Lady,
Cannot with too much zeale of rites be done,
They are ſo ſacred.

sp.speaker: PEC.
sp.l: But my Reputation.
May ſuffer, and the worſhip of my family,
When by ſo ſeruile meanes they both are ſought.

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: You are a noble, young, free, gracious Lady,
And would be euery bodie, in your bounty,
But you muſt not be ſo. They are a few
That know your merit, Lady, and can valew't.
Your ſelfe ſcarce vnderſtands your proper powers.
They are all-mighty, and that wee your ſeruants,
That haue the honour here to ſtand ſo neere you,
Know; and can vſe too. All this Nether-world
Is yours, you command it, and doe ſway it,
The honour of it, and the honeſty,
The reputation, I, and the religion,
(I was about to ſay, and had not err'd)
Is Queene Pecunia's. For that ſtile is yours,
If mortals knew your Grace, or their owne good.

sp.speaker: MOR.
sp.l: Pleaſe your Grace to retire.

sp.speaker: BAN.
sp.l: I feare your Grace
Hath ta'ne too much of the ſharpe ayre.

sp.speaker: PEC.
sp.l: O no!
I could endure to take a great deale more
(And with my conſtitution, were it left)
Vnto my choice, what thinke you of it, Statute?

sp.speaker: STA.
sp.l: A little now and then does well, and keepes
Your Grace in your complexion.

sp.speaker: BAN.
sp.l: And true temper.

sp.speaker: MOR.
sp.l: But too much Madame, may encreaſe cold rheumes,
Nouriſh catarrhes, greene ſickneſſes, and agues,
And put you in conſumption.

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: Beſt to take
Aduice of your graue women, Noble Madame,
They know the ſtate o'your body, and ha'ſtudied
Your Graces health.

sp.speaker: BAN.
sp.l: And honour. Here'll be viſitants,
Or Suitors by and by; and 'tis not fit
They find you here.

sp.speaker: STA.
sp.l: 'Twill make your Grace too cheape
To giue them audience preſently.

sp.speaker: MOR.
sp.l: Leaue your Secretary,
To anſwer them.

sp.speaker: PEC.
sp.l: Waite you here, Broker.

sp.speaker: BRO.
sp.l: I ſhal Madame.
And doe your Graces truſts with diligence.
WHat luck's this? I am come an inch too late,
Doe you heare Sir? Is your worſhip o'the family
Vnto the Lady Pecunia?

sp.speaker: BRO.
sp.l: I ſerue her Grace, Sir,
Aurelia Clara Pecunia, the Infanta.

sp.speaker: PYE.
sp.l: Has ſhe all thoſe Titles, and her Grace beſides,
I muſt correct that ignorance and ouer-ſight,
Before I doe preſent. Sir, I haue drawne
A Pedigree for her Grace, though yet a Nouice
In that ſo noble ſtudy.

sp.speaker: BRO.
sp.l: A Herald at Armes?

sp.speaker: PYE.
sp.l: No Sir, a Purſiuant, my name is Pyed-mantle.

sp.speaker: BRO.
sp.l: Good Maſter Pyed-mantle.

sp.speaker: PYE.
sp.l: I haue deduc'd her.—

sp.speaker: BRO.
sp.l: From all the Spaniſh Mines in the Weſt-Indi'es,
I hope: for ſhe comes that way by her mother,
But, by her Grand-mother, ſhe's Dutches of Mines.

sp.speaker: PYE.
sp.l: From mans creation I haue brought her.

sp.speaker: BRO.
sp.l: No further?
Before Sr, long before, you haue done nothing elſe,
Your Mines were before Adam, ſearch your Office,
Rowle fiue and twenty, you will finde it ſo,
I ſee you are but a Nouice, Maſter Pyed-mantle.
If you had not told mee ſo.

sp.speaker: PYE.
sp.l: Sir, an apprentiſe
In armoiry. I haue read the Elements,
And Accidence, and all the leading bookes,
And I haue, now, vpon me a great ambition,
How to be brought to her Grace, to kiſſe her hands.

sp.speaker: BRO.
sp.l: Why, if you haue acquaintance with Miſtreſſe Statute,
Or Miſtreſſe Band, my Ladies Gentlewomen,
They can induce you. One is a Iudges Daughter,
But ſomewhat ſtately; th'other Miſtreſſe Band,
Her father's but a Scriuener, but ſhee can
Almoſt as much with my Lady, as the other,
Eſpecially, if Roſe Waxe the Chambermaid
Be willing. Doe you not know her, Sir, neither?

sp.speaker: PYE.
sp.l: No in troth Sir.

sp.speaker: BRO.
sp.l: She's a good plyant wench,
And eaſie to be wrought, Sir, but the Nurſe
Old mother Mortgage, if you haue a Tenement,
Or ſuch a morſell? though ſhee haue no teeth,
Shee loues a ſweet meat, any thing that melts
In her warme gummes, ſhe could command it for you
On ſuch a trifle, a toy. Sir, you may ſee,
How for your loue, and this ſo pure complexion,
(A perfect Sanguine) I ha' ventured thus,
The ſtraining of a ward, opening a doore
Into the ſecrets of our family:

sp.speaker: PYE.
sp.l: I pray you let mee know, Sir, vnto whom
I am ſo much beholden; but your name.

sp.speaker: BRO.
sp.l: My name is Broker, I am Secretary,
And Vſher, to her Grace.

sp.speaker: PYE.
sp.l: Good Maſter Broker!

sp.speaker: BRO.
sp.l: Good Mr. Pyed-mantle.

sp.speaker: PYE.
sp.l: Why? you could do me,
If you would, now, this fauour of your ſelfe.

sp.speaker: BRO.
sp.l: Truely, I thinke I could: but if I would,
I hardly ſhould, without, or Miſtreſſe Band,
Or Miſtreſſe Statute, pleaſe to appeare in it.
Or the good Nurſe I told you of, Miſtreſſe Mortgage.
We know our places here, wee mingle not
One in anothers ſphere, but all moue orderly,
In our owne orbes; yet wee are all Concentricks.

sp.speaker: PYE.
sp.l: Well, Sir, I'll waite a better ſeaſon.

sp.speaker: BRO.
sp.l: Doe,
And ſtudy the right meanes, get Miſtreſſe Band
Broker makes a mouth at him. He ieeres him againe. Old Peny-boy leaps To vrge on your behalfe, or little Waxe.

sp.speaker: PYE.
sp.l: I haue a hope, Sir, that I may, by chance,
Light on her Grace, as ſhe's taking the ayre:

sp.speaker: BRO.
sp.l: That ayre of hope, has blaſted many an ayrie
Of Caſtrills like your ſelfe: Good Maſter Pyed-mantle,

sp.speaker: P.SE.
sp.l: Well ſaid, Maſter Secretary, I ſtood behinde
And heard thee all. I honor thy diſpatches.
If they be rude, vntrained it our method
And haue not ſtudied the rule, diſmiſſe 'hem quickly,
Where's Lickfinger my Cooke? that vnctuous raſcall?
Hee'll neuer keepe his houre, that veſſell of kitchinſtuffe!
HEere hee is come, Sir.

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: Pox vpon him kidney,
Alwaies too late!

sp.speaker: LIC.
sp.l: To wiſh 'hem you, I confeſſe,
That ha'them already.

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: What?

sp.speaker: LIC.
sp.l: The pox!

sp.speaker: P.SE.
sp.l: The piles,
The plague, and all diſeaſes light on him,
Knowes not to keepe his word. I'ld keepe my word ſure!
I hate that man that will not keepe his word,
When did I breake my word?

sp.speaker: LIC.
sp.l: Or I, till now?
And 'tis but halfe an houre.

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: Halfe a yeere:
To mee that ſtands vpon a minute of time.
I am a iuſt man, I loue ſtill to be iuſt.

sp.speaker: LIC.
sp.l: Why? you thinke I can runne like light-foot Ralph,
Or keep a wheele-barrow, with a ſayle in towne here,
To whirle me to you: I haue loſt two ſtone
Of ſuet i'the ſeruice poſting hither,
You might haue followed me like a watering pot,
And ſeene the knots I made along the ſtreet;
My face dropt like the ſkimmer in a fritter panne,
And my whole body, is yet (to ſay the truth)
A roſted pound of butter, with grated bread in't!He ſweepes his face.

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: Belieue you, he that liſt. You ſtay'd of purpose,
To haue my veniſon ſtinke, and my fowle mortify'd,
That you might ha' 'hem—

sp.speaker: LIc.
sp.l: A ſhilling or two cheaper,
That's your iealouſie.

sp.speaker: P.SE.
sp.l: Perhaps it is.
Will you goe in, and view, and value all?
Yonder is veniſon ſent mee! fowle! and fiſh!
In ſuch abundance! I am ſicke to ſee it!
I wonder what they meane! I ha' told 'hem of it!
To burthen a weake ſtomacke! and prouoke
A dying appetite! thruſt a ſinne vpon me
I ne'r was guilty of! nothing but gluttony!
Groſſe gluttony! that will vndoe this Land!

sp.speaker: LIC.
sp.l: And bating two i'the hundred.

sp.speaker: P.SE.
sp.l: I, that ſame's
A crying ſinne, a fearefull damn'd deuice,
Eats vp the poore, deuoures 'hem—

sp.speaker: LIC.
sp.l: Sir, take heed
What you giue out.

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: Againſt your graue great Solons?
Numae Pompilij, they that made that Law?
To take away the poore's inheritance?
It was their portion: I will ſtand to't.
And they haue rob'd 'hem of it, plainly rob'd 'hem,
I ſtill am a iuſt man, I tell the truth.
When moneies went at Ten i'the hundred, I,
And ſuch as I, the ſeruants of Pecunia,
Could ſpare the poore two out of ten, and did it,
How ſay you, Broker?

sp.speaker: (LIC.
sp.l: Ask your Eccho)

sp.speaker: BRO.
sp.l: You did it.

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: I am for Iuſtice, when did I leaue Iuſtice?
We knew 'twas theirs, they'had right and Title to't.
Now—

sp.speaker: LIC.
sp.l: You can ſpare 'hem nothing.

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: Very little,

sp.speaker: LIC.
sp.l: As good as nothing.

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: They haue bound our hands
With their wiſe ſolemne act, ſhortned our armes.

sp.speaker: LIC.
sp.l: Beware thoſe worſhipfull eares, Sir, be not ſhortned,
And you play Crop i the fleete, if you vſe this licence.

sp.speaker: P.SE.
sp.l: What licence, Knaue? Informer?

sp.speaker: LIC.
sp.l: I am Lickfinger,
Your Cooke.

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: A ſaucy Iacke you are, that's once;
VVhat ſaid I, Broker?

sp.speaker: BRO.
sp.l: Nothing that I heard, Sir.

sp.speaker: LIC.
sp.l: I know his gift, hee can be deafe when he liſt.

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: Ha' you prouided me my buſhell of egges?
I did beſpeake? I doe not care how ſtale,
Or ſtincking that they be; let 'hem be rotten:
For ammunition here to pelt the boyes,
That breake my windowes?

sp.speaker: LIC.
sp.l: Yes Sir, I ha' ſpar'd 'hem
Out of the cuſtard politique for you, the Maiors.

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: 'Tis well, goe in, take hence all that exceſſe,
Make what you can of it, your beſt: and when
I haue friends, that I inuite at home, prouide mee
Such, ſuch, and ſuch a diſh, as I beſpeake;
One a • a time, no ſuperfluitie.
Or if you haue it not, returne mee money;
You know my waies.

sp.speaker: LIC.
sp.l: They are a little crooked.

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: How knaue?

sp.speaker: LIC.
sp.l: Becauſe you do indent.

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: 'Tis true, Sir,
I do indent you ſhall returne me money.

sp.speaker: LIC.
sp.l: Rather then meat, I know it: you are iuſt ſtill.

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: I loue it ſtill. And therefore if you ſpend
The red-Deere pyes i'your houſe, or ſell'hem forth, Sir,
Caſt ſo, that I may haue their coffins all,
Return'd here, and pil'd vp: I would be thought
To keepe ſome kind of houſe.

sp.speaker: LIC.
sp.l: By the mouldie ſignes?

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: And then remember meat for my two dogs:
Fat flaps of mutton; kidneyes; rumps of veale;
Good plentious ſcraps; my maid ſhall eat the reliques.

sp.speaker: LIC.
sp.l: VVhen you & your dogs haue din'd. A ſweet reuerſion.

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: VVho's here? my Courtier? and my little Doctor?
My Muſter-Maſter? and what Plouer's that
They haue brought to pull?

sp.speaker: BRO.
sp.l: I know not, ſome green Plouer.
I'le find him out.

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: Doe, for I know the reſt,
They are the Ieerers, mocking, flouting Iackes.

div.head: ACT. II.
SCENE. IV.
FITTON. PENI-BOY. SE. ALMANACH. SHVNFIELD. MADRIGAL. LICK-FINGER. BROKER.

sp.l: HOw now old Money-Bawd? w'are come—

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: To ieere me,
As you were wont, I know you.

sp.speaker: ALM.
sp.l: No, to giue thee
Some good ſecurity, and ſee Pecunia.

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: What is't?

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Our ſelues.

sp.speaker: ALM.
sp.l: Wee • l be one bound for another.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: This noble Doctor here.

sp.speaker: ALM.
sp.l: This worthy Courtier.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: This Man o' war, he was our Muſter-Maſter.

sp.speaker: ALM.
sp.l: But a Sea-Captaine now, braue Captaine Shun-field. He holds vp his noſe.

sp.speaker: SHVN.
sp.l: You ſnuffe the ayre now, as the ſcent diſpleas'd you?

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Thou needſt not feare him man, his credit is ſound,

sp.speaker: ALM.
sp.l: And ſeaſon • d too, ſince he tooke ſalt at Sea.

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: I doe not loue pickl • d ſecurity,
Would I had one good Freſh-man in for all;
For truth is, you three ſtinke.

sp.speaker: SHV.
sp.l: You are a Rogue,

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: I thinke I am, but I will lend no money
On that ſecurity, Captaine.

sp.speaker: ALM.
sp.l: Here's a Gentleman,
A Freſh-man i'the world, one Maſter Madrigall.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Of an vntainted credit; what ſay you to him?

sp.speaker: SHV.
sp.l:
Hee's gone me thinkes, where is he? Madrigall?
Madrigall ſteps aſide with Broker.

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: H • has an odde ſinging name, is he an Heyre?

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: An Heyre to a faire fortune,

sp.speaker: ALM.
sp.l: And full hopes:
A dainty Scholler, and a pretty Poët!

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: Y'aue ſaid enough. I ha' no money, Gentlemen,
An' he goe to't in ryme once, not a penny.

sp.speaker: SHV.
sp.l: Why, hee's of yeares, though he haue little beard. He ſnuffes againe.

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: His beard has time to grow. I haue no money:
Let him ſtill dable in Poetry. No Pecunia
Is to be ſeene.

sp.speaker: ALM.
sp.l: Come, thou lou'ſt to be coſtiue
Still i' thy curt'ſie; but I haue a pill,
A golden pill to purge away this melancholly.

sp.speaker: SHV.
sp.l: Tis nothing but his keeping o'the houſe here,
With his two drowſie doggs.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: A drench of ſacke
At a good tauerne, and a fine freſh pullet,
Would cure him.

sp.speaker: LIC.
sp.l: Nothing but a yong Haire in white-broth,
I know his diet better then the Doctor.

sp.speaker: SHV.
sp.l: What Lick-finger? mine old hoſt of Ram-Alley?
You ha' ſome mereat here.

sp.speaker: ALM.
sp.l: Some doſſer of Fiſh
Or Fowle to fetch of.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: An odde bargaine of Veniſon,
To driue.

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: Will you goe in, knaue?

sp.speaker: LIC.
sp.l: I muſt needs,
You ſee who driues me, gentlemen.

sp.speaker: ALM.
sp.l: Not the diuell.

sp.speaker: FIT,
sp.l: Hee may be in time, hee is his Agent, now.

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: You are all cogging Iacks, a Couy o' wits,
The Ieerers, that ſtill call together at meales:
Or rather an Airy, for you are birds of prey:
Peny-boy thruſts him in. And flie at all, nothing's too bigge or high for you.
And are ſo truely fear'd, but not belou'd
One of another: as no one dares breake
Company from the reſt, leſt they ſhould fall,
Vpon him abſent.

sp.speaker: ALM.
sp.l: O! the onely Oracle
That euer peept, or ſpake out of a dublet.

sp.speaker: SHV.
sp.l: How the rogue ſtinks, worſe then a Fiſhmonger ſleeues!

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Or Curriers hands!

sp.speaker: SHV.
sp.l: And ſuch a perboil'd viſage!

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: His face lookes like a Diers apron, iuſt!

sp.speaker: ALM.
sp.l: A ſodden head, and his whole braine a poſſit curd!

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: I, now you ieere, ieere on; I haue no money.

sp.speaker: ALM.
sp.l: I wonder what religion hee's of!

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: No certaine ſpecies ſure, A kinde of mule!
That's halfe an Ethnicke, halfe a Chriſtian!

sp.speaker: P. Se.
sp.l: I haue no monie, gentlemen.

sp.speaker: SHV.
sp.l: This ſtocke.
He has no ſenſe of any vertue, honour,
Gentrie or merit.

sp.speaker: P. Se.
sp.l: You ſay very right,
My meritorious Captaine, (as I take it!)
Merit will keepe no houſe, nor pay no houſe rent.
Will Miſtreſſe Merit goe to mercat, thinke you?
Set on the pot, or feed the family?
Will Gentry cleare with the Butcher? or the Baker?
Fetch in a Pheſſant, or a brace of Partridges,
From good-wife Poulter, for my Ladies ſupper.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: See! this pure rogue!

sp.speaker: P. Se.
sp.l: This rogue has money tho',
My worſhipfull braue Courtier has no money.
No, nor my valiant Captaine.

sp.speaker: SHV.
sp.l: Hang you raſcall.

sp.speaker: P. Se.
sp.l: Nor you, my learned Doctor. I lou'd you
Whil you did hold your practice, and kill tripe wiues.
And kept you to your vrinall; but ſince your thombes
Haue greas'd the Ephemerides, caſting figures,
And turning ouer for your Candle-rents,
And your twelue houſes in the Zodiacke:
With your Almutens, Alma cantaras,
Troth you ſhall cant alone for Peny-boy.

sp.speaker: SHV.
sp.l: I told you what we ſhould find him, a meere Bawd.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: A rogue, a cheater.

sp.speaker: P.Se.
sp.l: What you pleaſe, gentlemen,
I am of that humble nature and condition,
Neuer to minde your worſhips, or take notice
Of what you throw away, thus. I keepe houſe here
Like a lame Cobler, neuer out of doores,
With my two dogs, my friends; and (as you ſay)
Driue a quicke pretty trade, ſtill. I get money:
And as for Titles, be they Rogue, or Raſcall,
Or what your worſhips fancy, let 'hem paſſe
As tranſitory things; they're mine to day,
And yours to morrow.

sp.speaker: ALM.
sp.l: Hang thee dog.

sp.speaker: SHV.
sp.l: Thou curre.

sp.speaker: P. Se.
sp.l: You ſee how I doe bluſh, and am aſham'd
Of theſe large attributes? yet you haue no money.

sp.speaker: ALM.
sp.l: Well wolfe, Hyaena, you old pockie raſcall,
You will ha' the Hernia fall downe againe
Into your Scrotum, and I ſhall be ſent for.
I will remember then, that; and your Fiſtula
In ano, I cur'd you of.

sp.speaker: P. Se.
sp.l: Thanke your dog-leech craft.
They were 'holeſome piles, afore you meddl'd with'hem.

sp.speaker: ALM.
sp.l: What an vngratefull wretch is this?

sp.speaker: SHV.
sp.l: Hee minds
A curteſie no more, then London-bridge,
What Arch was mended laſt.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Hee neuer thinkes.
More then a logge, of any grace at Court,
A man may doe him: or that ſuch a Lord
Reach't him his hand.

sp.speaker: P. Se.
sp.l: O yes! if grace would ſtrike
The brewers Tally, or my good Lords hand,
Would quit the ſcores. But Sir, they will not doe it.
Here's a piece, my good Lord piece, doth all. He ſhewes a piece.
Goes to the Butehers. fetches in a muton,
Then to the Bakers, brings in bread, makes fires,
Gets wine, and does more reall Curteſies,
Then all my Lords, I know: My ſweet Lord peece!
You are my Lord, the reſt are cogging Iacks,
Vnder the Roſe.

sp.speaker: SHV.
sp.l: Rogue, I could beat you now,

sp.speaker: P. Se.
sp.l: True Captaine, if you durſt beat any other.
I ſhould belieue you, but indeed you are hungry;
You are not angry Captaine, if I know you
Aright; good Captaine. No, Pecunia,
Is to be ſeene, though Miſtreſſe Band would ſpeake,
Or little Bluſhet-Waxe, be ne'r ſo eaſie,
I'll ſtop mine eares with her, againſt the Syrens,
Court, and Philoſophy. God be wi • you, Gentlemen,
Prouide you better names. Pecunia is for you.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: What a damn'd Harpy it is? where's Madrigall?
Is he ſneek'd hence.

sp.speaker: SHV.
sp.l: Here he comes with Broker, Madrigall returnes.

l.hi: Pecunia's Secretary.

sp.speaker: ALM.
sp.l: He may doe ſome good
With him perhaps. Where ha you beene Madrigall?

sp.speaker: MAD.
sp.l: Aboue with my Ladies women, reading verſes.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: That was a fauour. Good morrow, Maſter Secretary.

sp.speaker: SHV.
sp.l: Good morrow, Maſter Vſher.

sp.speaker: ALM.
sp.l: Sir, by both
Your worſhipfull Titles, and your name Mas Broker.
Good morrow.

sp.speaker: MAD.
sp.l: I did aske him if hee were

l.hi: Amphibion Broker.

sp.speaker: SHV.
sp.l: Why?

sp.speaker: ALM.
sp.l: A creature of two natures,
Becauſe hee has two Offices.

sp.speaker: BRO.
sp.l: You may ieere,
You ha' the wits, young Gentlemen. But your hope
Of Helicon, will neuer carry it, heere,
With our fat family; we ha' the dulleſt,
Moſt unboar'd Eares for verſe amongſt our females.
I grieu'd you read ſo long, Sir, old Nurſe Mortgage,
Shee ſnoar'd i'the Chaire, and Statute (if you mark'd her)
Fell faſt a ſleepe, and Miſtreſſe Band, ſhee nodded,
But not with any conſent to what you read.
They muſt haue ſomwhat elſe to chinke, then rymes.
If you could make an Epitaph on your Land,
(Imagine it on departure) ſuch a Poem
Would wake 'hem, and bring Waxe to her true temper.

sp.speaker: MAD.
sp.l: I faith Sir, and I will try.

sp.speaker: BRO.
sp.l: 'Tis but earth,
Fit to make brickes and tyles of.

sp.speaker: SHV.
sp.l: Pocks vpon't
'Tis but for pots, or pipkins at the beſt.
If it would keepe vs in good tabacco pipes,

sp.speaker: BRO.
sp.l: 'Twere worth keeping.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Or in porc'lane diſhes
There were ſome hope.

sp.speaker: ALM.
sp.l: But this is a hungry ſoile,
And muſt be helpt.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Who would hold any Land
To haue the trouble to marle it.

sp.speaker: SHV.
sp.l: Not a gentleman.

sp.speaker: BRO.
sp.l: Let clownes and hyndes affect it, that loue ploughes,
And carts, and harrowes, and are buſie ſtill,
In vexing the dull element.

sp.speaker: ALM.
sp.l: Our ſweete Songster
Shall rarifie t into ayre.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: And you Mas. Broker
Shall haue a feeling.

sp.speaker: BRO.
sp.l: So it ſupple, Sir,
The nerues.

sp.speaker: MAD.
sp.l: O! it ſhall be palpable,
Make thee runne thorow a hoope, or a thombe-ring,
The noſe of a tabacco pipe, and draw
Thy ductile bones out, like a knitting needle,
To ſerue my ſubtill turnes.

sp.speaker: BRO.
sp.l: I ſhall obey Sir,
And run a thred, like an houre-glaſſe.

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: Where is Broker?
Are not theſe flies gone yet? pray quit my houſe,
I'le ſmoake you out elſe.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: O! the Prodigall!
Will you be at ſo much charge with vs, and loſſe?

sp.speaker: MAD.
sp.l: I haue heard you ha' offered Sir, to lock vp ſmoake,
And cauke your windores, ſpar up all your doores,
Thinking to keepe it a cloſe priſoner wi'you,
And wept, when it went out, Sir, at your chimney.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: And yet his eyes were dryer then a pummiſe.

sp.speaker: SHV.
sp.l: A wretched raſcall, that will binde about
The noſe of his bellowes, leſt the wind get out
When hee's abroad.

sp.speaker: ALM.
sp.l: Sweepes downe no cobwebs here,
But ſells 'hem for cut-fingers. And the ſpiders,
As creatures rear'd of duſt, and coſt him nothing,
To fat old Ladies monkeyes.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Hee has offer • d
To gather vp ſpilt water, and preſerue
Each haire falls from him to ſtop balls with all.

sp.speaker: SHV.
sp.l: A ſlaue, and an Idolater to Pecunia!

sp.speaker: P.SE.
sp.l: You all haue happy memories, Gentlemen,
In rocking my poore cradle. I remember too,
When you had lands, and credit, worſhip, friends,
I, and could giue ſecurity: now, you haue none,
Or will haue none right ſhortly. This can time,
And the viciſſitude of things. I haue
All theſe▪ and money too, and doe poſſeſſe 'hem,
And am right heartily glad of all our memories,
And both the changes.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Let vs leaue the viper.

sp.speaker: P.SE.
sp.l: Hee's glad he is rid of his torture, and ſo ſoone.
Broker, come hither, vp, and tell your Lady,
Shee muſt be readie preſently, and Statute,
Band, Mortgage, VVax. My prodigall young kinſman
Will ſtreight be here to ſee her; 'top of our houſe,
The flouriſhing, and flanting Peny-boy.
Wee were but three of vs in all the world,
My brother Francis, whom they call'd Franck Peny-boy,
Father to this: hee's dead. This Peny-boy,
Is now the heire! I, Richer Peny-boy,
Not Richard, but old Harry Peny-boy,
And (to make rime) cloſe, wary Peny-boy
I ſhall haue all at laſt, my hopes doe tell me.
Goe, ſee all ready; and where my dogs haue falted,
Remoue it with a broome, and ſweeten all
VVith a ſlice of iuniper, not too much, but ſparing,
VVe may be faultie our ſelues elſe, and turne prodigall,
In entertaining of the Prodigall.
Here hee is! and with him—what! a Clapper Dudgeon!
That's a good ſigne; to haue the begger follow him,
So neere at his firſt entry into fortune.

div.head: ACT. II.
SCENE. V.
PENY-BOY. IV. PENI-BOY. SEN. PICLOCK. CANTER.) BROKER. PECVNIA. STATVTE. BAND. WAX. MORTGAGE. hid in the ſtudy.

sp.l: HOw now old Vncle? I am come to ſee thee.
And the braue Lady, here, the daughter of Ophir,
They ſay thou keepſt.

sp.speaker: P.SE.
sp.l: Sweet Nephew, if ſhe were
The daughter o' the Sunne, ſhee's at your ſeruice,
And ſo am I, and the whole family,
Worſhipfull Nephew.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Sai'ſt thou ſo, deare Vncle?
Welcome my friends then: Here is, Domine Picklocke:
My man o'Law, ſollicits all my cauſes.
Followes my buſineſſe, makes, and compounds my quarrells,
Betweene my tenants and mee, ſowes all my ſtrifes,
And reapes them too, troubles the country for mee,
And vexes any neighbour, that I pleaſe.

sp.speaker: P.SE.
sp.l: But with commiſſion?

sp.speaker: P.IV.
sp.l: Vnder my hand & ſeale.

sp.speaker: P.Se.
sp.l: A worſhipfull place!

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: I thanke his worſhip for it.

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: But what is this old Gentleman?

sp.speaker: P.CA.
sp.l: A Rogue,
A very Canter, I Sir, one that maunds
Vpon the Pad, wee ſhould be brothers though:
For you are neere as wretched as my ſelfe,
You dare not vſe your money, and I haue none.

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: Not vſe my money, cogging Iacke, who vſes it
At better rates? lets it for more i'the hundred,
Then I doe, Sirrah?

sp.speaker: P.IV.
sp.l: Be not angry vncle.

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: What? to diſgrace me, with my Queene? as if
I did not know her valew.

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: Sir, I meant
Young Peny-boy is angyry. You durſt not to enioy it.

sp.speaker: P.SE.
sp.l: Hold your peace,
You are a Iacke.

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: Vncle, he ſhall be a Iohn,
And, you goe to that, as good a man as you are.
An' I can make him ſo, a better man,
Perhaps I will too. Come, let vs goe.

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: Nay, kinſman,
My worſhipfull kinſman; and the top of our houſe;
Doe not your penitent vncle that affront,
For a raſh word, to leaue his ioyfull threſhold,
Before you ſee the Lady that you long for.
The Venus of the time, and ſtate, Pecunia!
I doe perceiue, your bounty loues the man,
For ſome concealed vertue, that he hides
Vnder thoſe rags.

sp.speaker: P.CA.
sp.l: I owe my happineſſe to him,
The waiting on his worſhip, ſince I brought him
The happy Newes, welcome to all young heires.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Thou didſt indeed, for which I thanke thee yet,
Your Fortunate Princeſſe, Vncle, is long a comming.

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: She is not rigg'd, Sir, ſetting forth ſome Lady,
Will coſt as much as furniſhing a Fleete,

Here ſhe's come at laſt,
The ſtudy is open'd where ſhe ſit in ſtate.
Shee kiſſeth him. and like a Galley
Guilt i'the prow.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Is this Pecunia?

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: Vouchſafe my toward kinſman, gracious Madame,
The fauour of your hand.

sp.speaker: PEC.
sp.l: Nay, of my lips, Sir,
To him.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: She kiſſes like a mortall creature,
Almighty Madame, I haue long'd to ſee you.

sp.speaker: PEC.
sp.l: And I haue my deſire, Sir, to behold
That youth, and ſhape, which in my dreames and wakes,
I haue ſo oft contemplated, and felt
Warme in my veynes, and natiue as my blood.
When I was told of your arriuall here,
I felt my heart beat, as it would leape out,
In ſpeach; and all my face it was a flame,
But how it came to paſſe I doe not know.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: O! beauty loues to be more proud then nature,
That made you bluſh. I cannot ſatisfie
My curious eyes, by which alone I'am happy,
In my beholding you.

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: They paſſe the complement
Prettily well.

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: I, he does kiſſe her, I like him. He kiſſeth her.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: My paſſion was cleare contrary, and doubtfull,
I ſhooke for feare, and yet I danc'd for ioy,
I had ſuch motions as the Sunne-beames make
Againſt a wall, or playing on a water,
Or trembling vapour of a boyling pot—

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: That's not ſo good, it ſhould ha'bin a Crucible,
With molten mettall, ſhe had vnderſtood it.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: I cannot talke, but I can loue you, Madame.
Are theſe your Gentlewomen? I loue them too.
And which is miſtreſſe Statute? Miſtreſſe Band?
They all kiſſe cloſe, the laſt ſtucke to my lips.

sp.speaker: BRO.
sp.l: It was my Ladies Chamber-maid, ſoft-Waxe.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Soft lips ſhe has, I am ſure on't. Mother Mortgage,
I'll owe a kiſſe, till ſhe be yonger, Statute, He doubles the complement to them all.
Sweet Miſtreſſe Band, and honey, little VVaxe,
We muſt be better acquainted.

sp.speaker: STA.
sp.l: We are but ſeruants, Sir.

sp.speaker: BAND.
sp.l: But whom her Grace is ſo content to grace,
We ſhall obſerue.

sp.speaker: WAX.
sp.l: Aand with all fit reſpect.

sp.speaker: MOR.
sp.l: In our poore places.

sp.speaker: WAX.
sp.l: Being her Graces ſhadowes.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: A fine well-ſpoken family. What's thy name?

l.hi: Broker.

sp.speaker: P.IV.
sp.l: Me thinks my vncle ſhould not need thee;
Who is a crafty Knaue, enough, beleeue it.
Art thou her Graces Steward?

sp.speaker: BRO.
sp.l: No, her Vſher, Sir.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: What, o'the Hall? thou haſt a ſweeping face,
Thy beard is like a broome.

sp.speaker: BRO.
sp.l: No barren chin, Sir,
I am no Eunuch, though a Gentleman-Vſher.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Thou ſhalt goe with vs. Vncle, I muſt haue
My Princeſſe forth to day.

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: Whither you pleaſe, Sir,
You ſhall command her.

sp.speaker: PEC.
sp.l: I will doe all grace
To my new ſeruant.

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: Thanks vnto your bounty;
Old Peny-boy thankes her, but makes his condition. He is my Nephew, and my Chiefe, the Point,
Tip, Top, and Tuft of all our family!
But, Sir, condition'd alwaies, you returne
Statute, and Band home, with my ſweet, ſoft Waxe,
And my good Nurſe, here, Mortgage.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: O! what elſe?

sp.speaker: P.SE.
sp.l: By Broker.

sp.speaker: P.IV.
sp.l: Do not feare.

sp.speaker: P.SE.
sp.l: She ſhall go wi' you,
Whither you pleaſe, Sir, any where.

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: I ſee
A Money-Bawd, is lightly a Fleſh-Bawd, too.

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: Are you aduis'd? Now o'my faith, this Canter
Would make a good graue Burgeſſe in ſome Barne.

sp.speaker: P.IV.
sp.l: Come, thou ſhalt go with vs, vncle.

sp.speaker: P.CA.
sp.l: By no means, Sir.

sp.speaker: P.IV.
sp.l: We'll haue both Sack, and Fidlers.

sp.speaker: P.SE.
sp.l: I'll not draw
That charge vpon your worſhip.

sp.speaker: P.CA.
sp.l: He ſpeakes modeſtly,
And like an Vncle,

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: But Mas Broker, here,
He ſhall attend you, Nephew; her Graces Vſher,
And what you fancy to beſtow on him,
Be not too lauiſh, vſe a temperate bounty,
I'll take it to my ſelfe.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: I will be princely,
While I poſſeſſe my Princeſſe, my Pecunia.

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: Where is't you eat?

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Hard by, at Picklocks lodging.
Old Lickfinger's the Cooke, here in Ram-Alley.

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: He has good cheare; perhaps I'll come and ſee you.

sp.speaker: P. CAN.
sp.l: O, fie! an Alley, and a Cooks-ſhop, groſſe,
The Canter takes him aſide, and perſwades him,'T will ſauour, Sir, moſt rankly of 'hem both.
Let your meat rather follow you, to a tauerne.

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: A tauern's as vnfit too, for a Princeſſe.

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: No, I haue knowne a Princeſſe, and a great one,
Come forth of a tauerne.

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: Not goe in, Sir, though.

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: She muſt goe in, if ſhe came forth: the bleſſed
Pokahontas (as the Hiſtorian calls her
And great Kings daughters of Virginia)
Hath bin in womb of a tauerne; and beſides,
Your naſty Vncle will ſpoyle all your mirth,
And be as noyſome.

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: That's true.

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: No 'faith,
Dine in Apollo with Pecunia,
At braue Duke Wadloos, haue your friends about you,
And make a day on't.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Content 'ifaith:
Our meat ſhall be brought thither. Simon the King,
Will bid vs welcome.

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: Patron, I haue a ſuite.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: What's that?

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: That you will carry the Infanta,
To ſee the Staple', her Grace will be a grace,
To all the members of it.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: I will doe it:
And haue her Armes ſet vp there, with her Titles,
Aurelia Clara Pecunia, the Infanta.
And in Apollo. Come (ſweete Princeſſe) goe.

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: Broker, be careful of your charge.

sp.speaker: BRO.
sp.l: I warrant you.

div.head: The ſecond Intermeane after the ſecond Act.

sp.speaker: CENSVRE.
sp.p: Why, this is duller and duller! intolerable! ſcuruy! neither Diuel nor Foole in this Play! pray God, ſome on vs be not a witch, Goſsip, to foreſpeake the matter thus.

sp.speaker: MIRTH.
sp.p: I feare we are all ſuch, and we were old enough: But we are not all old enough to make one witch. How like you the Vice i'the Play.

sp.speaker: EXPECTATION.
sp.p: Which is he?

sp.speaker: MIR.
sp.p: Three or foure: old Couetouſneſſe, the ſordid Peny-boy, the Money-bawd, who is a fleſh-bawd too, they ſay.

sp.speaker: TATLE.
sp.p: But here is neuer a Fiend to carry him away. Beſides, he has neuer a wooden dagger! I'ld not giue a ruſh for a Vice, that has not a wooden dagger to ſnap at euery body he meetes.

sp.speaker: MIRTH.
sp.p: That was the old way, Goſsip, when Iniquity came in like Hokos Pokos, in a Iuglers ierkin, with falſe skirts, like the Knaue of Clubs! but now they are attir'd like men and women o' the time, the Vices, male and female! Prodigality like a young heyre, and his Miſtreſſe Money (whoſe fauours he ſcatters like counters) prank't vp like a prime Lady, the Infanta of the Mines.

sp.speaker: CEN.
sp.p: I, therein they abuſe an honorable Princeſſe, it is thought.

sp.speaker: MIRTH.
sp.p: By whom is it ſo thought? or where lies the abuſe?

sp.speaker: CEN.
sp.p: Plaine in the ſtiling her Infanta, and giuing her three names.

sp.speaker: MIRTH.
sp.p: Take heed, it lie not in the vice of your interpretation: what haue Aurelia, Clara, Pecunia to do with any perſon? do they any more, but expreſſe the property of Money, which is the daughter of earth, and drawne out of the Mines? Is there nothing to be call'd Infanta, but what is ſubiect to exception? Why not the Infanta of the Beggers? or Infanta o'the Gipſies? as well as King of Beggers, and King of Gipſies?

sp.speaker: CEN.
sp.p: Well, and there were no wiſer then I, I would ſow him in a ſack, and ſend him by ſea to his Princeſſe.

sp.speaker: MIRT.
sp.p: Faith, and hee heard you Cenſure, he would goe neere to ſticke the Aſſes eares to your high dreſsing, and perhaps to all ours for harkening to you.

sp.speaker: TATLE.
sp.p: By'r Lady but he ſhould not to mine, I would harken, and harken, and cenſure, if I ſaw cauſe, for th'other Princeſſe ſake Pokahontas, ſurnam'd the bleſſed, whom hee has abus'd indeed (and I doe cenſure him, and will cenſure him) to ſay ſhe came foorth of a Tauerne, was ſaid like a paltry Poet.

sp.speaker: MIRTH.
sp.p: That's but one Goſsips opinion, and my Goſsip Tatle's too! but what ſaies Expectation, here, ſhe ſits ſullen and ſilent.

sp.speaker: EXP.
sp.p: Troth I expect their Office, their great Office! the Staple, what it will be! they haue talk't on't, but wee ſee't not open yet; would Butter would come in, and ſpread it-ſelfe a little to vs.

sp.speaker: MIRTH.
sp.p: Or the butter-box, Buz, the Emiſſary.

sp.speaker: TATLE.
sp.p: When it is churn'd, and diſh't, we ſhall heare of it.

sp.speaker: EXP.
sp.p: If it be freſh and ſweet butter; but ſay it be ſower and wheyiſh.

sp.speaker: MIR.
sp.p: Then it is worth nothing, meere pot-butter, fit to be ſpent in ſuppoſitories, or greaſing coach-wheeles, ſtale ſtinking butter, and ſuch I feare it is, by the being barrell'd vp ſo long.

sp.speaker: EXPECTATION.
sp.p: Or ranke Iriſh butter.

sp.speaker: CEN.
sp.p: Haue patience Goſsips, ſay that contrary to our expectations it proue right, ſeaſonable, ſalt butter.

sp.speaker: MIR.
sp.p: Or to the time of yeer, in Lent, delicate Almond butter! I haue a ſweet tooth yet, and I will hope the beſt; and ſit downe as quiet, and calme as butter, looke as ſmooth, and ſoft as butter; be merry, and melt like butter; laugh and be fat like butter: ſo butter anſwer my expectation, and be not mad butter; If it be: It ſhall both Iuly and December ſee.
I ſay no more, But— Dixi.

div.head: TO THE READERS.
div.p: IN this following Act, the Office is open'd, and ſhew'n to the Prodigall, and his Princeſſe Pecunia, wherein the allegory, and purpoſe of the Author hath hitherto beene wholly miſtaken, and ſo ſiniſter an interpretation beene made, as if the ſoules of moſt of the Spectators had liu'd in the eyes and eares of theſe ridiculous Goſſips that tattle betweene the Acts. But hee prayes you thus to mend it. To conſider the Newes here vented, to be none of his Newes, or any reaſonable mans; but Newes made like the times Newes, (a weekly cheat to draw mony) and could not be fitter reprehended, then in raiſing this ridiculous Office of the Staple, wherin the age may ſee her owne folly, or hunger and thirſt after publiſh • d pamphlets of Newes, ſet out euery Saturday, but made all at home, & no ſyllable of truth in them: then which there cannot be a greater diſeaſe in nature, or a fouler ſcorne put vpon the times. And ſo apprehending it, you ſhall doe the Author, and your owne iudgement a courteſie, and perceiue the tricke of alluring money to the Office, and there cooz'ning the people. If you haue the truth, reſt quiet, and conſider that

epigraph.q: Ficta, voluptatis cauſa, ſint proxima veris.

div.head: ACT. III.
SCENE. I.
FITTON. CYMBAL, to them PICKLOCKE. REGISTER. CLERKE. THO: BARBER.

sp.l: YOu hunt vpon a wrong ſcent ſtill, and thinke
The ayre of things will carry 'hem, but it muſt
Be reaſon and proportion, not fine ſounds,
My couſin Cymball, muſt get you this Lady.
You haue entertain'd a petty-fogger here,
Picklocke, with truſt of an Emiſſaries place,
And he is, all, for the young Prodigall,
You ſee he has left vs.

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: Come, you doe not know him,
That ſpeake thus of him. He will haue a tricke,
To open vs a gap, by a trap-doore,
When they leaſt dreame on't. Here he comes. What newes?

sp.speaker: PICK.
sp.l: Where is my brother Buz? my brother Ambler?
The Regiſter, Examiner, and the Clerkes?
Appeare, and let vs muſter all in pompe,
For here will be the rich Infanta, preſently,
To make her viſit. Peny-boy the heyre,
My Patron, has got leaue for her to play
With all her traine, of the old churle, her Guardian.
Now is your time to make all court vnto her;
That ſhe may firſt but know, then loue the place,
And ſhew it by her frequent viſits here:
And afterwards, get her to ſoiourne with you.
She will be weary of the Prodigall, quickly.

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: Excellent newes!

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: And counſell of an Oracle!

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: How ſay you couſin Fitton?

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: brother Picklock,
I ſhall adore thee, for this parcell of tidings,
It will cry vp the credit of our Office,
Eternally, and make our Staple immortall!

sp.speaker: PICK.
sp.l: Looke your addreſſes, then, be faire and fit,
And entertaine her, and her creatures, too,
With all the migniardiſe, and quaint Careſſes,
You can put on 'hem.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Thou ſeem'ſt, by thy language,
No leſſe a Courtier, then a man o' Law.
I muſt embrace thee.

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: Tut, I am Vertumnus,
On euery change, or chance, vpon occaſion,
A true Chamaelion, I can colour for't.
I moue vpon my axell, like a turne-pike.
Fit my face to the parties, and become
Streight, one of them.

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: Sirs, vp, into your Desks,
And ſpread the rolls vpon the Table, ſo.
Is the Examiner ſet?

sp.speaker: REG.
sp.l: Yes, Sir.

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: Ambler, and Buz,
Are both abroad, now.

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: Wee'll ſuſtaine their parts.
No matter, let them ply the affayres without,
Fitton puts on the office cloake, and Cymbal the gowne. Let vs alone within, I like that well.
On with the cloake, and you with the Staple gowne,
And keep your ſtate, ſtoupe only to the Infanta;
We'll haue a flight at Mortgage, Statute, Band,
And hard, but we'll bring Wax vnto the retriue:
Each know his ſeuerall prouince, and diſcharge it.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Fitton is brought about. I do admire this nimble ingine, Picklock.

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: Cuz,
What did I ſay?

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: You haue rectified my errour!

div.head: ACT. III.
SCENE. II.
PENI-BOY. IV. P. CANTER. PECVNIA. STATVTE. BAND. MORTGAGE. WAX. BROKER. CVSTOMERS.

sp.l: BY your leaue, Gentlemen, what newes? good, good ſtill?
I' your new Office? Princeſſe, here's the Staple!
This is the Gouernor, kiſſe him, noble Princeſſe,
For my ſake. Thom, how is it honeſt Thom?
Hee tells Pecunia of Thom. How does thy place, and thou? my Creature, Princeſſe?
This is my Creature, giue him your hand to kiſſe,
He was my Barber, now he writes Clericus!
I bought this place for him, and gaue it him.

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: He ſhould haue ſpoke of that, Sir, and not you:
Two doe not doe one Office well.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: 'Tis true,
But I am loth to loſe my curteſies.

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: So are all they, that doe them, to vaine ends,
And yet you do loſe, when you pay you ſelues.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: No more o' your ſentences, Canter, they are ſtale,
We come for newes, remember where you are.
I pray thee let my Princeſſe heare ſome newes,
Good Maſter Cymbal.

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: What newes would ſhe heare?
Or of what kind, Sir?

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Any, any kind.
So it be newes, the neweſt that thou haſt,
Some newes of State, for a Princeſſe.

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: Read from Rome, there. Newes from Rome.

sp.speaker: THO.
sp.l: They write, the King of Spaine is choſen Pope.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: How?

sp.speaker: THO.
sp.l: And Emperor too, the thirtieth of February.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Is the Emperor dead?

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: No, but he has reſign'd, Newes of the Emperor, and Tilly.
And trailes a pike now, vnder Tilly.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: For pennance.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Theſe will beget ſtrange turnes in Chriſtendome!

sp.speaker: THO.
sp.l:
And Spinola is made Generall of the Ieſuits.
Newes of Spinola. The fifth Monarchy, vniting the Eccleſiaſticke and Secular power.
A plot of the houſe of Auſtria.
More of Spinola.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Stranger!

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Sir, all are alike true, and certaine.

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: All the pretence to the fifth Monarchy,
Was held but vaine, vntill the eccleſiaſtique,
And ſecular powers, were vnited, thus,
Both in one perſon.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: 'T has bin long the ayme
Of the houſe of Auſtria.

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: See but Maximilian.
His letters to the Baron of Boutterſheim,
Or Scheiter-huyſſen.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: No, of Liechtenſtein,
Lord Paul, I thinke.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: I haue heard of ſome ſuch thing.
Don Spinola made Generall of the Ieſuits!
A Prieſt!

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: O, no, he is diſpenc'd with all,
And the whole ſociety, who doe now appeare
The onely Enginers of Chriſtendome.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: They haue bin thought ſo long, and rightly too.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Witneſſe the Engine, that they haue preſented him,
To winde himſelfe with, vp, into the Moone:
And thence make all his diſcoueries!

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: Read on.

sp.speaker: THO.
sp.l: And Vittelleſco, he that was laſt Generall,
Being now turn'd Cooke to the ſociety,
Has dreſt his excellence, His Egges. ſuch a diſh of egges—

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: What potch'd?

sp.speaker: THO.
sp.l: No, powder'd.

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: All the yolke is wilde fire,
As he ſhall need beleaguer no more townes,
But throw his Egge in.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: It ſhall cleare conſume,
Palace, and place; demoliſh and beare downe,
All ſtrengths before it!

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: Neuer be extinguiſh'd!
Till all become one ruine!

sp.speaker: FI.
sp.l: And from Florence,

sp.speaker: THO.
sp.l: They write was found in Galileos ſtudy, Galilaeo's ſtudy.
A burning Glaſſe (which they haue ſent him too)
To fire any Fleet that's out at Sea—

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: By Mooneſhine, is't not ſo?

sp.speaker: THO.
sp.l: Yes, Sir, i'the water. The burning glaſſe, by Moon-ſhine.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: His ſtrengths will be vnreſiſtable, if this hold!
Ha'you no Newes againſt him, on the contrary?

sp.speaker: CLA.
sp.l: The Holanders Ecle. Yes, Sit, they write here, one Cornelius-Son,
Hath made the Hollanders an inuiſible Eele,
To ſwimme the hauen at Dunkirke, and ſinke all
