The ſhipping there.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Why ha'not you this, Thom?

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: Peny-boy will haue him change ſides: Becauſe he keeps the Pontificiall ſide.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: How, change ſides, Thom. 'Twas neuer in my thought
To put thee vp againſt our ſelues. Come downe,
Quickly.

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: Why, Sir?

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: I venter'd not my mony
Vpon thoſe termes: If he may change; why ſo.
I'll ha him keepe his owne ſide, ſure.

sp.speaker: FIT,
sp.l: Why, let him,
'Tis but writing ſo much ouer againe.

sp.speaker: P.IV.
sp.l: For that I'll beare the charge: There's two Pieces,

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Come, do not ſtick with the gentleman.

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: I'l take none Sir.
And yet he ſhall ha'the place.

sp.speaker: P.IV.
sp.l: They ſhall be ten, then,
though hee pay for it. Vp, Thom: and th'Office ſhall take 'hem. Keep your ſide, Thom.
Know your owne ſide, doe not forſake your ſide, Thom.

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: Read.

sp.speaker: THO.
sp.l: They write here one Cornelius-Son,
Hath made the Hollanders an inuiſible Eele,
To ſwimme the Hauen at Dunkirke, and ſinke all
The ſhipping there.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: But how is't done?

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: I'll ſhew you Sit.
It is an Automa, runnes vnderwater,
With a ſnug noſe, and has a nimble taile
Made like an auger, with which taile ſhe wrigles
Betwixt the coaſts of a Ship, and ſinkes it ſtreight.

sp.speaker: P.IV.
sp.l: Whence ha'you this newes.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: From a right hand I aſſure you,
The Eele-boats here, that lye before Queen-Hyth,
Came out of Holland.

sp.speaker: P.IV.
sp.l: A moſt braue deuice,
To murder their flat bottomes.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: I doe grant you:
Spinola's new proiect: an army in cork-ſhooes. But what if Spinola haue a new Proiect:
To bring an army ouer in corke-ſhooes,
And land them, here, at Harwich? all his horſe
Are ſhod with corke, and foureſcore pieces of ordinance,
Mounted vpon cork-carriages, with bladders,
In ſtead of wheeles to runne the paſſage ouer
At a ſpring-tide.

sp.speaker: P.IV.
sp.l: Is't true?

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: As true as the reſt.

sp.speaker: P.IV.
sp.l: He'll neuer leaue his engines: I would heare now
Some curious newes.

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: As what?

sp.speaker: P.IV.
sp.l: Magick, or Alchimy
Or flying i'the ayre, I care not what.

sp.speaker: CLA.
sp.l: They write from Libtzig (reuerence to your eares)
Extraction of farts The Art of drawing farts out of dead bodies,
Is by the Brotherhood of the Roſie Croſſe,
Produc'd vnto perfection, in ſo ſweet
And rich a tincture—

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: As there is no Princeſſe,
But may perfume her chamber with th'extraction.

sp.speaker: P.IV.
sp.l: There's for you. Princeſſe.

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: What, a fart for her?

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: The perpetuall Motion. I meane the ſpirit.

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: Beware how ſhe reſents it.

sp.speaker: P.IV.
sp.l: And what haſt thou, Thom?

sp.speaker: THO.
sp.l: The perpetuall Motion,
Is here found out by an Alewife in Saint Katherines,
At the ſigne o' the dancing Beares.

sp.speaker: P.IV.
sp.l: What, from her tap?
I'll goe ſee that, or elſe I'll ſend old Canter.
He can make that diſcouery.

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: Yes, in Ale.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Let me haue all this Newes, made vp, and ſeal'd.

sp.speaker: REG.
sp.l: The people preſſe vpon vs, pleaſe you, Sir, The Regiſter offers him a roome.
Withdraw with your faire Princeſſe. There's a roome
Within, Sir, to retyre too.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: No, good Regiſter,
We'll ſtand it out here, and obſerue your Office; The Office call'd the houſe of fame.
What Newes it iſſues.

sp.speaker: REG.
sp.l: 'Tis the houſe of fame, Sir,
Where both the curious, and the negligent;
The ſcrupulous, and careleſſe; wilde, and ſtay'd;
The idle, and laborious; all doe meet,
To taſt the Cornu copiae of her rumors,
Which ſhe, the mother of ſport, pleaſeth to ſcatter
Among the vulgar: Baites, Sir, for the people!
And they will bite like fiſhes.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Let's ſee't.

sp.speaker: DOP.
sp.l:
Ha' you in your prophane Shop, any Newes
1. Cu • A ſheſt. baptiſt.
O'the Saints at Amſterdam?

sp.speaker: REG.
sp.l: Yes, how much would you?

sp.speaker: DOP.
sp.l: Six peny worth.

sp.speaker: REG.
sp.l: Lay your mony down, read, Thomas.

sp.speaker: THO.
sp.l: The Saints do write, they expect a Prophet. ſhortly, Prophet Baal expected in Holland.
The Prophet Baal, to be ſent ouer to them,
To calculate a time, and halfe a time,
And the whole time, according to Naömetry.

sp.speaker: P.IV.
sp.l: What's that?

sp.speaker: THO.
sp.l: The meaſuring o'the Temple: a Cabal
Found out but lately, and ſet out by Archie,
Or ſome ſuch head, of whoſe long coat they haue heard, Archie mourn'd then.
And being black, deſire it.

sp.speaker: DOP.
sp.l: Peace be with them!

sp.speaker: REG.
sp.l: So there had need, for they are ſtill by the eares
One with another.

sp.speaker: DOP.
sp.l: It is their zeale.

sp.speaker: REG.
sp.l: Moſt likely.

sp.speaker: DOP.
sp.l: Haue you no other of that ſpecies?

sp.speaker: REG.
sp.l: Yes,
But dearer, it will coſt you a ſhilling.

sp.speaker: DOP.
sp.l: Verily,
There is a' nine-pence, I will ſhed no more.

sp.speaker: REG.
sp.l: Not; to the good o'the Saints?

sp.speaker: DOP.
sp.l: I am not ſure,
Tha • man is good.

sp.speaker: REG.
sp.l: Read, from Conſtantinople,
〈◊〉 penny'orth.

sp.speaker: THO.
sp.l: They giue out here, the grand Signior The great Turk turn'd Chriſtian.
Is ••• tainely turn'd Chriſtian, and to cleare
The controuerſie 'twixt the Pope and him,
Which is the Antichriſt; he meanes to viſit
The Church at Amſterdam, this very Sommer,
And quit all marks o'the beaſt.

sp.speaker: DOP.
sp.l: Now ioyfull tydings.
Who brought in this? Which Emiſſary?

sp.speaker: REG.

l.hi: Buz.

sp.l: Your countrey-man.

sp.speaker: DOP.
sp.l: Now, bleſſed be the man,
And his whole Family, with the Nation.

sp.speaker: REG.
sp.l: Yes, for Amboyna, and the Iuſtice there!
This is a Doper, a ſhe Anabaptiſt!
Seale and deliuer her her newes, diſpatch.

sp.speaker: C. 2.
sp.l: 2. Cuſt. Ha'you any newes from the Indies? any mirac
Done in Iapan, by the Ieſuites? or in China?

sp.speaker: CLA.
sp.l: A Coloney o • Cookes ſent ouer to conuert the Canniballs. No, but we heare of a Colony of cookes
To be ſet a ſhore o' the coaſt of America,
For the conuerſion of the Caniballs,
And making them good, eating Cbriſtians.
Here comes the Colonell that vndertakes it.

sp.speaker: C. 2.
sp.l: Who? captaine Lickfinger?

sp.speaker: LIC.
sp.l: 3. Cust. By Colonel Lickfinger. Newes, newes my boyes!
I am to furniſh a great feaſt to day,
And I would haue what newes the Office affords.

sp.speaker: CLA.
sp.l: We were venting ſome of you, of your new project,

sp.speaker: REG.
sp.l: Afore 'twas paid for, you were ſomewhat too haſty.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: What Lickfinger! wilt thou conuert the Caniballs,
With ſpit and pan Diuinity?

sp.speaker: LIC.
sp.l: Sir, for that
I will not vrge, but for the fire and zeale
To the true cauſe; thus I haue vndertaken:
With two Lay-brethren, to my ſelfe, no more,
O • eo the broach, th'other o'the boyler,
In one ſixe months, and by plaine cookery,
No magick to't, but old Iaphets phyſicke,
The father of the Europaean Arts,
To make ſuch ſauces for the Sauages,
And cookes their meats, with thoſe inticing ſteemes,
As it would make our Caniball-Chriſtians,
Forbeare the mutuall eating one another,
Which they doe doe, more cunningly, then the wilde
Anthropophagi; that ſnatch onely ſtrangers,
Like my old Patrons dogs, there.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: O, my Vncles!
Is dinner ready, Lickfinger?

sp.speaker: LIC.
sp.l: When you pleaſe, Sir.
I was beſpeaking but a parcell of newes,
To ſtrew out the long meale withall, but 't ſeemes
You are furniſh'd here already.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: O, not halfe!

sp.speaker: LIC.
sp.l: What Court-newes is there? any Proclamations,
Or Edicts to come forth.

sp.speaker: THO.
sp.l: Yes, there is one.
That the Kings Barber has got, for aid of our trade:
Whereof there is a manifeſt decay.
T • let long hayre runne to •• ed, to ſow bald pates. A Precept for the wearing of long haire,
To runne to ſeed, to ſow bald pates withall,
And the preſeruing fruitfull heads, and chins,
To help a miſtery, almoſt antiquated.
Such as are bald and barren beyond hope,
Are to be ſeparated, and ſet by
For Vſhers, to old Counteſſes.

sp.speaker: LIC.
sp.l: And Coachmen.
To mount their boxes, reuerently, and driue,
Like Lapwings, with a ſhell vpo' their heads.
Thorow the ſtreets. Ha' you no Newes o'the Stage?
They'll aske me abou new Playes, at dinner time.
And I ſhould be as dumbe as a fiſh.

sp.speaker: THO.
sp.l: O! yes.

There is a Legacy left to the Kings Players,
Spalato's Legacy to the Players.
Both for their various ſhifting of their Scene,
And dext'rous change o'their perſons to all ſhapes,
And all diſguiſes: by the right reuerend
Archbiſhop of Spalato.

sp.speaker: LIC.
sp.l: He is dead,
That plai'd him!

sp.speaker: THO.
sp.l: Then, h'has loſt his ſhare o' the Legacy.

sp.speaker: LIC.
sp.l: What newes of Gundomar?

sp.speaker: THO.
sp.l: A ſecond Fiſtula,
Or an excoriation (at the leaſt)
For putting the poore Engliſh-play, was writ of him, Gundomar's vſe of the game at Cheſſe, or Play ſo called.
To ſuch a ſordid vſe, as (is ſaid) he did,
Of cleanſing his poſterior's.

sp.speaker: LIC.
sp.l: Iuſtice! Iuſtice!

sp.speaker: THO.
sp.l: Since when, he liues condemn'd to his ſhare, at Bruxels.
And there ſits filing certaine politique hinges,
To hang the States on, h'has heau'd off the hookes.

sp.speaker: LIC.
sp.l: What muſt you haue for theſe?

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Thou ſhalt pay nothing,
But reckon 'hem in i'the bill. There's twenty pieces, Hee giues 20. pieces, to the Office. Doubles it.
Her Grace beſtowes vpon the Office, Thom,
Write thou that downe for Newes.

sp.speaker: REG.
sp.l: We may well do't,
We haue not many ſuch.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: There's twenty more,
If you ſay ſo; my Princeſſe is a Prineceſſe!
And put that too, vnder the Office Seale.

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: If it will pleaſe your Grace to ſoiourne here, Cymbal takes Pecunia aſide, courts and wooes her, to the Office.
And take my roofe for couert, you ſhall know
The rites belonging to your blood, and birth,
Which few can apprehend: theſe ſordid ſeruants,
Which rather are your keepers, then attendants,
Should not come neere your preſence. I would haue
You waited on by Ladies, and your traine
Borne vp by perſons of quality, and honour,
Your meat ſhould be ſeru'd in with curious dances,
And ſet vpon the boord, with virgin hands,
Tun • d to their voices; not a diſh remou'd,
But to the Muſicke, nor a drop of wine,
Mixt, with his water, without Harmony,

sp.speaker: PEC.
sp.l: You are a Courtier, Sir, or ſomewhat more;
That haue this tempting language!

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: I'm your ſeruant,
Exellent Princeſſe, and would ha' you appeare
That, which you are. Come forth State, and wonder,
Of theſe our times, dazle the vulgar eyes.
And ſtrike the people blind with admiration.

sp.speaker: P.CAN.
sp.l: Why, that's the end of wealth! thruſt riches outward,
And remaine beggers within: contemplate nothing
But the vile ſordid things of time, place, money,
And let the noble, and the precious goe,
Vertue and honeſty; hang 'hem; poore thinne membranes
Of honour; who reſpects them? O, the Fates!
How hath all iuſt, true reputation fall'n,
Fitton hath beene courting the waiting-women, this whole, and is ieered by them. Since money, this baſe money 'gan to haue any!

sp.speaker: BAN.
sp.l: Pitty, the Gentleman is not immortall.

sp.speaker: WAX.
sp.l: As he giues out, the place is, by deſcription.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: A very Paradiſe, if you ſaw all, Lady.

sp.speaker: WAX.
sp.l: I am the Chamber-maid, Sir, you miſtake,
My Lady may ſee all.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Sweet Miſtreſſe Statute, gentle Miſtreſſe Band,
And Mother Mortgage, doe but get her Grace
To ſoiourne here.—

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: I thanke you gentle Waxe,

sp.speaker: MOR.
sp.l: If it were a Chattell, I would try my credit.

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: So it is, for terme of life, we count it ſo.

sp.speaker: STA.
sp.l: She meanes, Inheritance to him, and his heyres:
Or that he could aſſure a State, of yeeres:
I'll be his Statute-Staple, Statute-Merchant,
Or what he pleaſe.

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: He can expect no more.

sp.speaker: BAN.
sp.l: His couſin Alderman Security,
That he did talke of ſo, e'en now—

sp.speaker: STA.
sp.l: Who, is
The very broch o'the bench, gem o'the City.

sp.speaker: BAN.
sp.l: He and his Deputy, but aſſure his life
For one ſeuen yeeres.

sp.speaker: STA.
sp.l: And ſee what we'll doe for him,
Vpon his ſcarlet motion.

sp.speaker: BAN.
sp.l: And old Chaine,
That drawes the city-eares.

sp.speaker: WAX.
sp.l: When he ſayes nothing,
But twirles it thus.

sp.speaker: STA.
sp.l: A mouing Oratory!

sp.speaker: BAN.
sp.l: Dumb Rethoricke, and ſilent eloquence!
As the fine Poet ſaies!

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Come, they all ſcorne vs,
Doe you not ſee't? the family of ſcorne!

sp.speaker: BRO.
sp.l: Doe not belieue him! gentle Maſter Picklocke,
They vnderſtood you not: the Gentlewomen,
They thought you would ha' my Lady ſoiourne, with you,
And you deſire but now and then, a viſit?

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: Yes, if ſhe pleas'd, Sir, it would much aduance
Vnto the Office, her continuall reſidence!
(I ſpeake but as a member)

sp.speaker: BRO.
sp.l: 'Tis inough.
I apprehend you. And it ſhall goe hard,
But I'll ſo worke, as ſome body ſhall worke her!

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: 'pray you change with our Maſter, but a word about it.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Well, Lickfinger, ſee that our meat be ready,
Thou haſt Newes inough.

sp.speaker: LIC.
sp.l: Something of Bethlem Gabor,
And then I'm gone.

sp.speaker: THO.
sp.l: We heare he has deuis'd
Bethlem Gabors Drum. A Drumme, to fill all Chriſtendome with the ſound:
But that he cannot drawe his forces neere it,
To march yet, for the violence of the noiſe.
And therefore he is faine by a deſigne,
To carry 'hem in the ayre, and at ſome diſtance,
Till he be married, then they ſhall appeare.

sp.speaker: LIC.
sp.l: Or neuer; well, God b'w'you (ſtay, who's here?)
A little of the Duke of Bauier, and then— The Duke of Bauier.

sp.speaker: CLA.
sp.l: H'has taken a gray habit, and is turn'd
The Churches Millar, grinds the catholique griſt
With euery wind: and Tilly takes the toll.

sp.speaker: CVS. 4.
sp.l: Ha'you any newes o'the Pageants to ſend downe? 4. Cuſt. The Pageants.
Into the ſeuerall Counties. All the countrey
Expected from the city moſt braue ſpeeches,
Now, at the Coronation.

sp.speaker: LIC.
sp.l: It expected
More then it vnderſtood: for, they ſtand mute,
Poore innocent dumb things; they are but wood.
As is the bench and blocks, they were wrought on, yet
If May-day come, and the Sunne ſhine, perhaps,
They'll ſing like Memnons Statue, and be vocall.

sp.speaker: CVS. 5.
sp.l: Ha'you any Foreſt-newes?

sp.speaker: THO.
sp.l: None very wild, Sir, 5. Cuſt. The new Parke in the Forreſt of Fooles.
Some tame there is, out o' the Forreſt of fooles,
A new Parke is a making there, to ſeuer
Cuckolds of Antler, from the Raſcalls. Such,
Whoſe wiues are dead, and haue ſince caſt their heads,
Shall remaine Cuckolds-pollard.

sp.speaker: LIC.
sp.l: I'll ha' that newes.

sp.speaker: CVS. 1.
sp.l: And I. 2. And I. 3. And I. 4. And I. 5. And I.

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l:
Sir, I deſire to be excus'd; and, Madame:
Peny-boy would inuite the Maſter of the Office
I cannot leaue my Office, the firſt day.
My Couſin Fitton here, ſhall wait vpon you.
And Emiſſary Picklocke.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: And Thom: Clericus?

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: I cannot ſpare him yet, but he ſhall follow you,
When they haue ordered the Rolls. Shut vp th'Office,
When you ha' done, till two a clocke.
BY your leaue, Clerkes,
Where ſhall we dine to day? doe you know? the Ieerers.

sp.speaker: ALM.
sp.l: Where's my fellow Fitton?

sp.speaker: THO.
sp.l: New gone forth.

sp.speaker: SHV.
sp.l: Cannot your Office tell vs, what braue fellowes
Doe eat together to day, in towne, and where?

sp.speaker: THO.
sp.l: Yes, there's a Gentleman, the braue heire, yong Peny-boy.
Dines in Apollo.

sp.speaker: MAD.
sp.l: Come, let's thither then,
I ha' ſupt in Apollo!

sp.speaker: ALM.
sp.l: With the Muſes?

sp.speaker: MAD.
sp.l: No, Sir.
But with two Gentlewomen, call'd, the Graces.

sp.speaker: ALM.
sp.l: They' were euer three in Poetry.

sp.speaker: MAD.
sp.l: This was truth,

sp.speaker: THO.
sp.l: Sir, Maſter Fitton's there too!

sp.speaker: SHV.
sp.l: All the better!

sp.speaker: ALM.
sp.l: We may haue a ieere, perhaps.

sp.speaker: SHV.
sp.l: Yes, you'll drink, Doctor.
(If there be any good meat) as much good wine now,
As would lay vp a Dutch Ambaſſador.

sp.speaker: THO.
sp.l: If he dine there, he's ſure to haue good meat,
For, Lickfinger prouides the dinner.

sp.speaker: ALM.
sp.l: Who?
The glory o'the Kitchin? that holds Cookery,
A trade from Adam? quotes his broths, and ſallads?
And ſweares he's not dead yet, but tranſlated
In ſome immortall cruſt, the paſt of Almonds?

sp.speaker: MAD.
sp.l: The ſame. He holds no man can be a Poet,
That is not a good Cooke, to know the palats,
And ſeuerall taſtes o'the time. He drawes all Arts
Out of the Kitchin, but the Art of Poetry,
which he concludes the ſame with Cookery.

sp.speaker: SHV.
sp.l: Tut, he maintaines more hereſies then that.
He'll draw the Magiſterium from a minc'd-pye,
And preferre Iellies, to your Iulips, Doctor.

sp.speaker: ALM.
sp.l: I was at an Olla Podrida of his making,
Was a braue piece of cookery! at a funerall!
But opening the pot-lid, he made vs laugh,
who'had wept all day! and ſent vs ſuch a tickling
Into our noſtrills, as the funerall feaſt
Had bin a wedding-dinner.

sp.speaker: SHV.
sp.l: Gi'him allowance,
And that but moderate, he will make a Syren
Sing i'the Kettle, ſend in an Arion,
In a braue broth, and of a watry greene,
Iuſt the Sea-colour, mounted on the backe
Of a growne Cunger, but, in ſuch a poſture,
As all the world would take him for a Dolphin.

sp.speaker: MAD.
sp.l: Hee's a rare fellow, without queſtion! but
He holds ſome Paradoxes.

sp.speaker: ALM.
sp.l: I, and Pſeudodoxes.
Mary, for moſt, he's Orthodox i'the Kitchin.

sp.speaker: MAD.
sp.l: And knowes the Clergies taſt!

sp.speaker: ALM.
sp.l: I, and the Layties!

sp.speaker: SHV.
sp.l: You thinke not o'your time, we'll come too late,
If we go not preſently.

sp.speaker: MAD.
sp.l: Away then.

sp.speaker: SHV.
sp.l: Sirs,
You muſt get o'this newes, to ſtore your Office,
VVho dines and ſups i' the towne? where, and with whom?
'Twill be beneficiall: when you are ſtor'd;
And as we like our fare, we ſhall reward you.

sp.speaker: CLA.
sp.l: A hungry trade, 'twill be.

sp.speaker: THO.
sp.l: Much like D. Humphries,
But, now and then, as th'holeſome prouerb ſaies,
'Twill obſonare famem ambulando.

sp.speaker: CLA.
sp.l: Shut vp the Office: gentle brother Thomas.

sp.speaker: THO.
sp.l: Brother, Nathaniel, I ha'the wine for you.
I hope to ſee vs, one day, Emiſſaries.

sp.speaker: CLA.
sp.l: Why not? S'lid, I deſpaire not to be Maſter!
HOw now? I thinke I was borne vnder Hercules ſtarre! He is ſtarted with Broker's comming back.
Nothing but trouble and tumult to oppreſſe me?
Why come you backe? where is your charge?

sp.speaker: BRO.
sp.l: I ha' brought
A Gentleman to ſpeake with you?

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: To ſpeake with me?
You know 'tis death for me to ſpeake with any man.
What is he? ſet me a chaire.

sp.speaker: BRO.
sp.l: He's the Maſter
Of the great Office.

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: What?

sp.speaker: BRO.
sp.l: The Staple of Newes,
A mighty thing, they talke Six thouſand a yeere.

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: Well bring your ſixe in. Where ha' you left Pecunia?

sp.speaker: BRO.
sp.l: Sir, in Apollo, they are ſcarce ſet.

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: Bring ſixe.

sp.speaker: BRO.
sp.l: Here is the Gentleman.

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: He muſt pardon me,
I cannot riſe, a diſeas'd man.

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: By no meanes, Sir,
Reſpect your health, and eaſe.

sp.speaker: P.SE.
sp.l: It is no pride in me!
But paine, paine; what's your errand, Sir, to me?Hee ſends Broker backe.
Broker, returne to your charge, be Argus-eyed,
Awake, to the affaire you haue in hand,
Serue in Apollo, but take heed of Bacchus.
Goe on, Sir.

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: I am come to ſpeake with you.

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: 'Tis paine for me to ſpeake, a very death,
But I will heare you!

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: Sir, you haue a Lady,
That ſoiournes with you.

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: Ha? I am ſomewhat ſhort He pretends infirmity.
In my ſenſe too—

l.hi: Pecunia.

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: O' that ſide,
Very imperfect, on—

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: Whom I would draw
Oftner to a poore Office, I am Maſter of—

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: My hearing is very dead, you muſt ſpeake quicker.

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: Or, if it pleaſe you, Sir, to let her ſoiourne
In part with me; I haue a moyety
We will diuide, halfe of the profits.

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: Ha?
I heare you better now, how come they in?
Is it a certaine buſineſſe, or a caſuall?
For I am loth to ſeeke out doubtfull courſes,
Runne any hazardous paths, I loue ſtreight waies,
A iuſt, and vpright man! now all trade totters.
The trade of money, is fall'n, two i'the hundred.
That was a certaine trade, while th' age was thrifty,
And men good husbands, look'd vnto their ſtockes,
Had their mindes bounded; now the publike Riot
Proſtitutes all, ſcatters away in coaches,
In foot-mens coates, and waiting womens gownes,
They muſt haue veluet hanches (with a pox)
Hee talkes vehemently and aloud. Now taken vp, and yet not pay the vſe;
Bate of the vſe? I am mad with this times manners.

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: You ſaid e'en now, it was death for you to ſpeake.

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: I, but an anger, a iuſt anger, (as this is)
Puts life in man. Who can endure to ſee
The fury of mens gullets, and their groines?
Is mou'd more and more. What fires, what cookes, what kitckins might be ſpar'd?
What Stewes, Ponds, Parks, Coupes, Garners, Magazines?
What veluets, tiſſues, ſcarfes, embroyderies?
And laces they might lacke? They couet things—
Superfluous ſtill; when it were much more honour
They could want neceſſary! What need hath Nature
Of ſiluer diſhes? or gold chamber-pots?
Of perfum'd napkins? or a numerous family,
To ſee her eate? Poore, and wiſe ſhe, requires
Meate onely; Hunger is not ambitious:
Say, that you were the Emperour of pleaſures,
The great Dictator of faſhions, for all Europe,
And had the pompe of all the Courts, and Kingdomes,
Laid forth vnto the ſhew? to make your ſelfe
Gaz'd, and admir'd at? You muſt goe to bed,
And take your naturall reſt: then, all this vaniſheth.
Your brauery was but ſhowen; 'twas not poſſeſt:
While it did boaſt it ſelfe, it was then periſhing.

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: This man has healthfull lungs.

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: All that exceſſe
Appear'd as little yours, as the Spectators.
It ſcarce fills vp the expectation
Of a few houres, that entertaines mens liues.

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: He has the monopoly of ſole-ſpeaking.
He is angry. Why, good Sir? you talke all.

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: Why ſhould I not?
Is it not vnder mine owne roofe? my feeling?

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: But I came hete to talk with you.

sp.speaker: P. S.
sp.l: Why, an'I will not
Talke with you, Sir? you are anſwer'd, who ſent for you?

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: Bids him get out of his houſe. No body ſent for me—

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: But you came, why then
Goe, as you came, heres no man holds you, There,
There lies your way, you ſee the doore.

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: This's ſtrange!

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: 'Tis my ciuility, when I doe not relliſh
The party, or his buſineſſe. Pray you be gone, Sir.
I'll ha' no venter in your Ship, the Office
Your Barke of Six, if'twere ſixteene, good, Sir,

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: Cymbal railes at him. He ieeres him. You are a rogue.

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: I thinke I am Sir, truly.

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: A Raſcall, and a money-bawd.

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: My ſurnames:

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: A wretched Raſcall!

sp.speaker: P. S.
sp.l: You will ouerflow—
And ſpill all.

sp.speaker: CYM.
sp.l: Caterpiller, moath,
Horſe-leach, and dung-worme—

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: Still you loſe your labor.
I am a broken veſſell, all runnes out:
A ſhrunke old Dryfat. Fare you well, good Sixe.

div.head: The third Intermeane after the third Act.

sp.speaker: CENSVRE.
sp.p: A notable tough Raſcall! this old Peny-boy! right City-bred!

sp.speaker: MIRTH.
sp.p: In Siluer-ſtreete, the Region of money, a good ſeat for a Vſurer.

sp.speaker: TATLE.
sp.p: He has rich ingredients in him, I warrant you, if they were extracted, a true receit to make an Alderman, an' he were well wrought vpon, according to Art.

sp.speaker: EXP.
sp.p: I would faine ſee an Alderman in chimia! that is a treatiſe of Aldermanity truely written.

sp.speaker: CEN.
sp.p: To ſhew how much it differs from Vrbanity.

sp.speaker: MIRTH.
sp.p: I, or humanity. Either would appeare in this Peny boy, an' hee were rightly diſtill'd. But how like you the newes? you are gone from that.

sp.speaker: CEN.
sp.p: O, they are monſtrous! ſcuruy! and ſtale! and too exotick! ill cook'd! and ill diſh'd!

sp.speaker: EXP.
sp.p: They were as good, yet, as butter could make them!

sp.speaker: TAT.
sp.p: In a word, they were beaſtly buttered! he ſhall neuer come o' my bread more, nor my in mouth, if I can helpe it. I haue had better newes from the bake-houſe, by ten thouſand parts, in a morning: or the conduicts in Weſtminſter! all the newes of Tutle-ſtreet, and both the Alm'ries! the two Sanctuaries long, and round Wool-ſtaple! with Kings-ſtreet, and Chanon-row to boot!

sp.speaker: MIRTH.
sp.p: I, my Goſsip Tatle knew what fine ſlips grew in Gardiners-lane; who kiſt the Butchers wife with the Cowes-breath; what matches were made in the bowling-Alley, and what bettes wonne and loſt; how much grieft went to the Mill and what beſides: who coniur'd in Tutle-fields, and how many? when they neuer came there. And which Boy rode vpon Doctor Lambe, in the likeneſſe of a roaring Lyon, that runne away with him in his teeth, and ha's not deuour'd him yet.

sp.speaker: TAT.
sp.p: Why, I had it from my maid Ioane Heare-ſay: and ſhee had it from a limbe o'the ſchoole, ſhee ſaies, a little limbe of nine yeere old; who told her, the Maſter left out his coniuring booke one day, and hee found it, and ſo the Fable came about. But whether it were true, or no, we Goſsips are bound to beleeue it, an't be once out, and a foot: how ſhould wee entertaine the time elſe, or finde our ſelues in faſhionable diſcourſe, for all companies, if we do not credit all, and make more of it, in the reporting?

sp.speaker: CEN.
sp.p:
For my part, I beleeue it: and there were no wiſer then I, I would haue ne'er a cunning Schoole-Maſter in England. I meane a Cunning-Man, a Schoole-Maſter; that is a Coniurour, or a Poet, or that had any acquaintance with a Poet. They make all their ſchollers Play-boyes! Is't not a fine ſight, to ſee all our children made Enterluders? Doe wee pay our money for this? wee ſend them to learne their
Grammar, and their Terence, and they learne their play-books? well, they talke, we ſhall haue no more Parliaments (God bleſſe vs) but an'wee haue, I hope, Zeale-of-the-land Buzy, and my Goſsip, Rabby Trouble-truth will ſtart vp, and ſee we ſhall haue painfull good Miniſters to keepe Schoole, and Catechiſe our youth, and not teach 'hem to ſpeake Playes, and Act Fables of falſe newes, in this manner, to the ſuper- • exation of Towne and Countrey, with a wanion.

div.head: ACT. IIII.
SCENE. I.
PENY-BOY. IV. FITTON. SHVNFIELD. ALMANACK. MADRIGAL. CANTER. PICKLOCKE.

sp.l:
•
Ome, Gentlemen, let's breath from healths a while.
This Lickfinger has made vs a good dinner,
For our Pecunia: what ſhal's doe with our ſelues,
While the women water? and the Fidlers eat?

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Let's ieere a little.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Ieere? what's that?

sp.speaker: SHV.
sp.l: Expect, Sr.

sp.speaker: ALM.
sp.l: We firſt begin with our ſelues, & then at you,

sp.speaker: SHV.
sp.l: A game we vſe.

sp.speaker: MAD.
sp.l: We ieere all kind of perſons
We meete withall, of any rancke or quality,
And if we cannot ieere them, we ieere our ſelues.

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: A pretty ſweete ſociety! and a gratefull!

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: 'Pray let's ſee ſome.

sp.speaker: SHV.
sp.l: Haue at you, then Lawyer.
They ſay, there was one of your coate in Bet'lem, lately,

sp.speaker: ALM.
sp.l: I wonder all his Clients were not there.

sp.speaker: MAD.
sp.l: They were the madder ſort.

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: Except, Sir, one
Like you, and he made verſes.

sp.speaker: FIT.

l.hi: Madrigall,

sp.l: A ieere.

sp.speaker: MAD.
sp.l: I know.

sp.speaker: SHV.
sp.l: But what did you doe, Lawyer?
When you made loue to Miſtreſſe Band, at dinner.

sp.speaker: MAD.
sp.l: Why? of an Aduocate, he grew the Clyent.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Well play'd, my Poet.

sp.speaker: MAD.
sp.l: And ſhew'd the Law of nature
Was there aboue the Common-Law.

sp.speaker: SHV.
sp.l: Quit, quit,

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Call you this ieering? I can play at this,
'Tis like a Ball at Tennis.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Very like,
But we were not well in.

sp.speaker: ALM.
sp.l: 'Tis indeed, Sir.
When we doe ſpeake at volley, all the ill
We can one of another.

sp.speaker: SHV.
sp.l: As this morning,
(I would you had heard vs) of the Rogue your Vncle.

sp.speaker: ALM
sp.l: That Mony-bawd.

sp.speaker: MAD.
sp.l: We call'd him a Coat-card
O'the laſt order.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: What's that? a Knaue?

sp.speaker: MAD.
sp.l: Some readings haue it ſo, my manuſcript

Doth ſpeake it,
• arlet.

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: And your ſelfe a Foole
O'the firſt ranke, and one ſhall haue the leading
O'the right-hand file, vnder this braue Commander.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: What ſaiſt thou, Canter?

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: Sir, I ſay this is
A very wholeſome exerciſe, and comely.
Like Lepers, ſhewing one another their ſcabs.
Or flies feeding on vlcers.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: What Newes Gentlemen?
Ha' you any newes for after dinner? me thinks
We ſhould not ſpend our time vnprofitably.

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: They neuer lie, Sir, betweene meales, 'gainſt ſupper
You may haue a Bale or two brought in.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: This Canter,
Is an old enuious Knaue!

sp.speaker: ALM.
sp.l: A very Raſcall!

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: I ha' mark'd him all this meale, he has done nothing
But mocke, with ſcuruy faces, all wee ſaid.

sp.speaker: ALM.
sp.l: A ſupercilious Rogue! he lookes as if
He were the Patrico—

sp.speaker: MAD.
sp.l: Or Arch-prieſt o'Canters,

sp.speaker: SHV.
sp.l: Hee's ſome primate metropolitan Raſcall,
Our ſhot-clog makes ſo much of him.

sp.speaker: ALM
sp.l: The Law,
And he does gouerne him

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: What ſay you, Gentlemen?

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: We ſay, we wonder not, your man o' Law,
Should be ſo gracious wi'you; but how it comes,
This Rogue, this Canter!

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: O, good words.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: A fellow
That ſpeakes no language—

sp.speaker: ALM.
sp.l: But what gingling Gipſies,
And Pedlers trade in—

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: And no honeſt Chriſtian
Can vnderſtand—

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: Why? by that argument,
You all are Canters, you, and you, and you, He ſpeakes to all the Ieerers.
All the whole world are Canters, I will proue it
In your profeſsions.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: I would faine heare this,
But ſtay, my Princeſſe comes, prouide the while,
I'll call for't anone. How fares your Grace?

div.head: ACT. IIII.
SCENE. II.
LICKFINGER. PECVNIA. STATVTE. BAND. VVAXE. to them.

sp.l: I hope the fare was good.

sp.speaker: PEC.
sp.l:
Yes, Lickfinger,
Lickfinger is challeng'd by Madrigal of an argument.
And we ſhall thanke you for't and reward you.

sp.speaker: MAD.
sp.l: Nay, I'll not loſe my argument, Lickfinger;
Before theſe Gentlemen, I affirme,
The perfect, and true ſtraine of poetry,
Is rather to be giuen the quicke Celler,
Then the fat Kitchin.

sp.speaker: LIC.
sp.l: Heretique, I ſee
Thou art for the vaine Oracle of the Botle.
The hogſhead, Triſmegiſtus, is thy Pegaſus.
Thence flowes thy Muſes ſpring, from that hard hoofe▪
Seduced Poet, I doe ſay to thee,
A Boyler, Range, and Dreſſer were the Fountaines,
Of all the knowledge in the vniuerſe.
And they 'are the Kitchins, where the Maſter-Cooke—
(Thou doſt not know the man, nor canſt thou know him,
Till thou haſt ſeru'd ſome yeeres in that deepe ſchoole,
That's both the Nurſe and Mother of the Arts,
And hear'ſt him read, interpret, and demonſtrate!)
A Maſter-Cooke! Why, he's the man o' men,
For a Profeſſor! he deſignes, he drawes,
He paints, he carues, he builds, he fortifies,
Makes Citadels of curious fowle and fiſh,
Some he dri-diſhes, ſome motes round with broths.
Mounts marrowbones, cuts fifty angled cuſtards,
Reares bulwark pies, and for his outerworkes
He raiſeth Ramparts of immortall cruſt;
And teacheth all the Tacticks, at one dinner:
What Rankes, what Files, to put his diſhes in;
The whole Art Military. Then he knowes,
The influence of the Starres vpon his meats,
And all their ſeaſons, tempers, qualities,
And ſo to fit his reliſhes, and ſauces,
He has Nature in a pot, 'boue all the Chymiſts,
Or airy brethren of the Roſie-croſſe.
He is an Architect, an Inginer,
A Souldiour, a Phyſician, a Philoſopher,
A generall Mathematician.

sp.speaker: MAD.
sp.l: It is granted.

sp.speaker: LIC.
sp.l: And that you may not doubt him, for a Poet—

sp.speaker: ALM.
sp.l: This fury ſhewes, if there were nothing elſe!
And 'tis diuine! I ſhall for euer hereafter,
Admire the wiſedome of a Cooke!

sp.speaker: BAN.
sp.l: And we, Sir!

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: O, how my Princeſſe drawes me, Peny-boy is courting his Princeſſe all the while. with her lookes,
And hales me in, as eddies draw in boats,
Or ſtrong Charybdis ſhips, that ſaile too neere
The ſhelues of Loue! The tydes of your two eyes!
Wind of your breath, are ſuch as ſucke in all,
That doe approach you!

sp.speaker: PEC.
sp.l: Who hath chang'd my ſeruant?

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Your ſelfe, who drinke my blood vp with your beames,
As doth the Sunne, the Sea! Pecunia ſhines
More in the world then he: and makes it Spring
Where e'r ſhe fauours! 'pleaſe her but to ſhow
Her melting wreſts, or bare her yuorie hands,
She catches ſtill! her ſmiles they are Loue's fetters!
Her breſts his apples! her teats Stawberries!
Where Cupid (were he preſent now) would cry
Fare well my mothers milke, here's ſweeter Nectar!
Helpe me to praiſe Pecunia, Gentlemen:
She's your Princeſſe, lend your wits, They all beginne the encomium of Pecunia.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: A Lady,
The Graces taught to moue!

sp.speaker: ALM.
sp.l: The Houres did nurſe!

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Whoſe lips are the inſtructions of all Louers!

sp.speaker: ALM.
sp.l: Her eyes their lights, and riualls to the Starres!

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: A voyce, as if that Harmony ſtill ſpake!

sp.speaker: ALM.
sp.l: And poliſh'd skinne, whiter then Venus foote!

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Young Hebes necke, or Iunoe's armes!

sp.speaker: ALM.
sp.l: A haire,
Large as the Mornings, and her breath as ſweete,
As meddowes after raine, and but new mowne!

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Laeda might yeeld vnto her, for a face!

sp.speaker: ALM.
sp.l: Hermione for breſts!

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Flora, for cheekes!

sp.speaker: ALM.
sp.l: And Helen for a mouth!

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Kiſſe, kiſſe 'hem, Princeſſe. She kiſſeth them.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: The pearle doth ſtriue in whiteneſſe, with her necke,

sp.speaker: ALM.
sp.l: But loſeth by it: here the Snow thawes Snow;
One froſt reſolues another!

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: O, ſhe has
A front too ſlippery to be look't vpon!

sp.speaker: ALM.
sp.l: And glances that beguile the ſeers eyes!

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Kiſſe, kiſſe againe, Againe. what ſaies my man o' warre?

sp.speaker: SHV.
sp.l: I ſay, ſhe's more, then Fame can promiſe of her.
A Theame, that's ouercome with her owne matter!
Praiſe is ſtrucke blind, and deafe, and dumbe with her!
Shee doth aſtoniſh Commendation!

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l:
Well pumpt i'faith old Sailor:
She kiſſeth Captaine Shunfield. kiſſe him too:
Though he be a ſlugge. What ſaies my Poet-ſucker!
He's chewing his Muſes cudde, I doe ſee by him.

sp.speaker: MAD.
sp.l: I haue almoſt done, I want but e'ne to finiſh.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: That's the 'ill luck of all his workes ſtill.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: What?

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: To beginne many works, but finiſh none;

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: How does he do his Miſtreſſe work?

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Imperfect.

sp.speaker: ALM.
sp.l: I cannot thinke he finiſheth that.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Let's heare▪

sp.speaker: MAD.
sp.l: It is a Madrigall, I affect that kind
Of Poem, much.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: And thence you ha' the name.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: It is his Roſe. He can make nothing elſe

sp.speaker: MAD.
sp.l: I made it to the tune the Fidlers play'd,
That we all lik'd ſo well.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Good, read it, read it.

sp.speaker: MAD.
sp.l: The Sunne is father of all mettalls, you know,
Siluer, and gold.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: I, leaue your Prologues, ſay!
As bright as is the Sunne her Sire,
Or Earth her mother, in her beſt atyre,
Or Mint, the Mid-wife, with her fire,
Comes forth her Grace!
The ſplendour of the wealthieſt Mines!

l.hi: The ſtamp, and ſtrength of all imperiall lines,
Both maieſty and beauty ſhines,
In her ſweet face!
Looke how a Torch, of Taper light,
Or of that Torches flame, a Beacon bright;

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: That Mint the Midwife does well.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: That's fairely said of Money.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Good!

sp.speaker: MAD.
sp.l: Now there, I want a line to finiſh, Sir.

l.hi: Or of that Beacons fire, Moone-light:

sp.speaker: MAD.

l.hi: So takes ſhe place!

sp.l: And then I haue a Saraband—

l.hi: She makes good cheare, ſhe keepes full boards,

sp.l: She holds a Faire of Knights, and Lords,

l.hi: A Mercat of all Offices,
And Shops of honour, more or leſſe.

sp.l: According to Pecunia's Grace,

l.hi: The Bride hath beauty, blood, and place,
The Bridegrome vertue, valour, wit,
And wiſedome, as he ſtands for it.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: 'Tis good.

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: He vrgeth her to kiſſe them all. Call in the Fidlers. Nicke, the boy ſhall ſing it,
Sweet Princeſſe, kiſſe him, kiſſe 'hem all, deare Madame,
And at the cloſe, vouchſafe to call them Couſins.

sp.speaker: PEC.
sp.l: Sweet Couſin Madrigall, and Couſin Fitton,
My Couſin Shunfield, and my learned Couſin.

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: Al-manach, though they call him Almanack.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Why, here's the Prodigall proſtitutes his Miſtreſſe!

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: And Picklocke, he muſt be a kinſman too.
My man o' Law will teach vs all to winne,
And keepe our owne. Old Founder.

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: Nothing, I Sir?
I am a wretch, a begger. She the fortunate.
Can want no kindred, wee, the poore know none.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Nor none ſhall know, by my conſent.

sp.speaker: ALM.
sp.l: Nor mine,

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Sing, boy, The boy ſings the ſong. ſtand here.

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: Look, look, how all their eyes
Dance i'their heads (obſerue) ſcatter'd with luſt!
At ſight o' their braue Idoll! how they are tickl'd,
With a light ayre! the bawdy Saruband!
They are a kinde of dancing engines all!
And ſet, by nature, thus, to runne alone
To euery ſound! All things within, withou them,
Moue, but their braine, and that ſtands ſtill! mere monſters
Here, in a chamber, of moſt ſubtill feet!
And make their legs in tune, paſſing the ſtreetes!
Theſe are the gallant ſpirits o'the age!
The miracles o'the time! that can cry vp
And downe mens wits! and ſet what rate on things
Their half-brain'd fancies pleaſe! Now pox vpon 'hem.
See how ſolicitouſly he learnes the Iigge,
As if it were a myſtery of his faith!

sp.speaker: SHV.
sp.l: A dainty ditty!

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: O, hee's a dainty Poet!
When he ſets to't!

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: And a dainty Scholler! They are all ſtruck with admiration.

sp.speaker: ALM.
sp.l: No, no great ſcholler, he writes like a Gentleman.

sp.speaker: SHV.
sp.l: Pox o'your Scholler.

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: Pox o'your diſtinction!
As if a Scholler were no Gentleman.
With theſe, to write like a Gentleman, will in time
Become, all one, as to write like an Aſſe,
Theſe Gentlemen? theſe Raſcalls! I am ſicke
Of indignation at 'hem.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: How doe you lik't, Sir?

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: 'Tis excellent!

sp.speaker: ALM.
sp.l: 'Twas excellently ſung!

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: A dainty Ayre!

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: What ſaies my Lickfinger?

sp.speaker: LIC.
sp.l: I am telling Miſtreſſe Band, and Miſtreſſe Statute,
What a braue Gentleman you are, and Waxe, here!
How much 'twere better, that my Ladies Grace,
Would here take vp Sir, and keepe houſe with you.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: What ſay they?

sp.speaker: STA.
sp.l: We could conſent, Sr, willingly.

sp.speaker: BAND.
sp.l: I, if we knew her Grace had the leaſt liking.

sp.speaker: WAX.
sp.l: We muſt obey her Graces will, and pleaſure.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: I thanke you, Gentlewomen, ply 'hem, Lickfinger.
Giue mother Mortgage, there—

sp.speaker: LIC.
sp.l: Her doze of Sacke.
I haue it for her, and her diſtance of Hum.

sp.speaker: PEC.
sp.l: Indeede therein, I muſt confeſſe, deare Couſin, The Gallants are all about Pecunia.
I am a moſt vnfortunate Princeſſe.

sp.speaker: ALM.
sp.l: And
You ſtill will be ſo, when your Grace may helpe it.

sp.speaker: MAD.
sp.l: Who'ld lie in a roome, with a cloſe-ſtoole, and garlick?
And kennell with his dogges? that had a Prince
Like this young Peny-boy, to ſoiourne with?

sp.speaker: SHV.
sp.l: He'll let you ha' your liberty—

sp.speaker: ALM.
sp.l: Goe forth,
Whither you pleaſe, and to what company—

sp.speaker: MAD.
sp.l: Scatter your ſelfe amongſt vs—

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Hope of Pernaſſus!
Thy Iuy ſhall not wither, nor thy Bayes,
Thou ſhalt be had into her Graces Cellar,
And there know Sacke, and Claret, all December,
Thy veine is rich, and we muſt cheriſh it.
Poets and Bees ſwarme now adaies, but yet
There are not thoſe good Tauernes, for the one ſort,
As there are Flowrie fields to feed the other.
Though Bees be pleas'd with dew, aske little Waxe
That brings the honey to her Ladyes hiue:
The Poet muſt haue wine. And he ſhall haue it.
BRoker? what Broker?

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Who's that? my Vncle!

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: I am abus'd, where is my Knaue? my Broker?

sp.speaker: LIC.
sp.l: Your Broker is laid out vpon a bench, yonder,
Sacke hath ſeaz'd on him, in the ſhape of ſleepe.

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: Hee hath beene dead to vs almoſt this houre.

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: This houre?

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: Why ſigh you Sr? 'cauſe he's at reſt?

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: It breeds my vnreſt.

sp.speaker: LIC.
sp.l: Will you take a cup
And try if you can ſleepe?

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: No, cogging Iacke,
Thou and thy cups too, periſh.

sp.speaker: SHV.
sp.l: O, the Sacke!He ſtrikes the Sacke out of his hand.

sp.speaker: MAD.
sp.l: The ſacke, the ſacke!

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: A Madrigall on Sacke!

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: Or rather an Elegy, for the Sacke is gone.

sp.speaker: PEC.
sp.l: VVhy doe you this, Sir? ſpill the wine, and raue?
For Brokers ſleeping?

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: VVhat through ſleepe, and Sacke,
My truſt is wrong'd: but I am ſtill awake,
Hee would haue Pecunia home. But ſhee refuſeth. And her Traine. To waite vpon your Grace, pleaſe you to quit
This ſtrange lewd company, they are not for you.

sp.speaker: PEC.
sp.l: No Guardian, I doe like them very well.

sp.speaker: P. SE
sp.l: Your Graces pleaſure be obſeru'd, but you
Statute, and Band, and Waxe, will goe with me.

sp.speaker: SAT.
sp.l: Truly we will not.

sp.speaker: BAN.
sp.l: VVe will ſtay, and wait here
Vpon her Grace, and this your Noble Kinſman.

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: Noble? how noble! who hath made him noble?

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: VVhy, my moſt noble money hath, or ſhall▪
My Princeſſe, here. She that had you but kept,
And treated kindly, would haue made you noble,
And wiſe, too: nay, perhaps haue done that for you,
An Act of Parliament could not, made you honeſt.
The truth is, Vncle, that her Grace diſlikes
Her entertainment: ſpecially her lodging.

sp.speaker: PEC.
sp.l: Nay, ſay her iaile. Neuer vnfortunate Princeſſe,
Was vs'd ſo by a Iaylor. Aske my women,
Band, you can tell, and Statute, how he has vs'd me,
Kept me cloſe priſoner, vnder twenty bolts—

sp.speaker: STA.
sp.l: And forty padlocks—

sp.speaker: BAN.
sp.l: All malicious ingines
A wicked Smith could forge out of his yron:
As locks, and keyes, ſhacles, and manacles,
To torture a great Lady.

sp.speaker: STA.
sp.l: H'has abus'd
Your Graces body.

sp.speaker: PEC.
sp.l: No, he would ha' done,
That lay not in his power: he had the vſe
Of our bodies, Band, and Waxe, and ſometimes Statutes:
But once he would ha'ſmother'd me in a cheſt,
And ſtrangl'd me in leather, but that you
Came to my reſcue, then, and gaue mee ayre.

sp.speaker: STA.
sp.l: For which he cramb'd vs vp in a cloſe boxe,
All three together, where we ſaw no Sunne
In one ſixe moneths.

sp.speaker: WAX.
sp.l: A cruell man he is!

sp.speaker: BAN.
sp.l: H'has left my fellow Waxe out, i'the cold,

sp.speaker: STA.
sp.l: Till ſhe was ſtiffe, as any froſt, and crumbl'd
Away to duſt, and almoſt loſt her forme.

sp.speaker: WAX.
sp.l: Much adoe to recouer me.

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: Women Ieerers!
Haue you learn'd too, the ſubtill facultie?
Come, I'll ſhew you the way home, if drinke,
Or, too full diet haue diſguis'd you.

sp.speaker: BAN.
sp.l: Troth,
We haue not any mind, Sir, of returne—

sp.speaker: STA.
sp.l: To be bound back to backe—

sp.speaker: BAN.
sp.l: And haue our legs
Turn'd in, or writh'd about—

sp.speaker: WAX.
sp.l: Or elſe diſplay'd—

sp.speaker: STA.
sp.l: Be lodg'd with duſt and fleas, as we were wont—

sp.speaker: BAN.
sp.l: And dyeted with dogs dung.

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: Why? you whores,
My bawds, my inſtruments, what ſhould I call you,
Man may thinke baſe inough for you?

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Heare you, vncle.
I muſt not heare this of my Princeſſe ſeruants,
And in Apollo, in Pecunia's roome,
Goe, get you downe the ſtaires: Home, to your Kennell,
As ſwiftly as you can. Conſult your dogges,
The Lares of your family; or beleeue it,
The fury of a foote-man, and a drawer
Hangs ouer you.

sp.speaker: SHV.
sp.l: Cudgell, and pot doe threaten
A kinde of vengeance.

sp.speaker: MAD.
sp.l: Barbers are at hand.

sp.speaker: ALM.
sp.l: Waſhing and ſhauing will enſue. They all threaten,

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: The Pumpe
Is not farre off; If't were, the ſinke is neere:
Or a good Iordan.

sp.speaker: MAD.
sp.l: You haue now no money,

sp.speaker: SHV.
sp.l: But are a Raſcall.

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: I am cheated, robb'd
Ieer'd by confederacy.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: No, you are kick'd
And vſed kindly, as you ſhould be.

sp.speaker: SHV.
sp.l: Spurn'd, And ſpurne him.
Kicke him, out. Hee exclaimes. From all commerce of men, who are a curre.

sp.speaker: ALM.
sp.l: A ſtinking dogge, in a dublet, with foule linnen.

sp.speaker: MAD.
sp.l: A ſnarling Raſcall, hence.

sp.speaker: SHV.
sp.l: Out.

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: Wel, remember,
I am coozen'd by my Couſin, and his whore!
Bane o'theſe meetings in Apollo!

sp.speaker: LIC.
sp.l: Goe, Sir,
One of his Dogges. You will be toſt like Block, in a blanket elſe.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Downe with him, Lickfinger.

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: Saucy Iacke away,
Pecunia is a whore.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Play him downe, Fidlers,
And drown his noiſe. Who's this!

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: O Maſter Pyed-mantle!
Pyed-mantle brings the Lady Pecunia her pedigree. BY your leaue, Gentlemen.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Her Graces Herald,

sp.speaker: ALM.
sp.l: No Herald yet, a Heraldet.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: What's that?

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: A Canter.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: O, thou ſaid'ſt thou'dſt ſproue vs all ſo!

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: Sir, here is one will proue himſelfe ſo, ſtreight,
So ſhall the reſt, in time.

sp.speaker: PEC.
sp.l: My Pedigree?
I tell you, friend, he muſt be a good Scholler,
Can my diſcent. I am of Princely race,
And as good blood, as any is i'the mines,
Runnes through my veines. I am, euery limb, a Princeſſe!
Dutcheſſe O' mynes, was my great Grandmother.
And by the Fathers ſide, I come from Sol.
My Grand-father was Duke of Or, and match'd
In the blood-royall of Ophyr.

sp.speaker: PYE.
sp.l: Here's his Coat.

sp.speaker: PEC.
sp.l: I know it, if I heare the Blazon.

sp.speaker: PYE.
sp.l: He beares
In a field Azure, a Sunne proper, beamy,
Twelue of the ſecond.

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: How farr's this from canting?

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Her Grace doth vnderſtand ti.

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: She can cant, Sr ▪

sp.speaker: PEC.
sp.l: What be theſe? Beſants?

sp.speaker: PYE.
sp.l: Yes, an't pleaſe your Grace.

sp.speaker: PEC.
sp.l: That is our Coat too, as we come from Or.
What line's this?

sp.speaker: PYE.
sp.l: The rich mynes of Potoſi.
The Spaniſh mynes i'the Weſt-Indies.

sp.speaker: PEC.
sp.l: This?

sp.speaker: PYE.
sp.l: The mynes o' Hungary, this of Barbary.

sp.speaker: PEC.
sp.l: But this, this little branch.

sp.speaker: PEC.
sp.l: The Welſh-myne that.

sp.speaker: PEC.
sp.l: I ha' Welſh-blood in me too, blaze, Sir, that Coat.

sp.speaker: PYE.
sp.l: She beares (an't pleaſe you) Argent, three leekes vert
In Canton Or, and taſſel'd of the firſt.

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: Is not this canting? doe you vnderſtand him?

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Not I, but it ſounds well, and the whole thing▪
Is rarely painted, I will haue ſuch a ſcrowle,
What ere it coſt me.

sp.speaker: PEC.
sp.l: VVell, at better leaſure,
We'll take a view of it, and ſo reward you.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Kiſſe him, ſweet Princeſſe, She kiſſeth. and ſtile him a Couſin.

sp.speaker: PEC.
sp.l: I will, if you will haue it. Couſin Pyed-mantle.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: I loue all men of vertue, from my Princeſſe,
Vnto my begger, here, old Canter, on,
On to thy proofe, whom proue you the next Canter?

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: The Doctor here, I will proceed with the learned.
VVhen he diſcourſeth of diſſection,
Or any point of Anatomy: that hee tells you,
Of Vena caua, and of vena porta,
The Meſeraicks, and the Meſenterium.
VVhat does hee elſe but cant? Or if he runne
To his Iudiciall Aſtrologie,
And trowle the Trine, the Quartile and the Sextile,
Platicke aſpect, and Partile, with his Hyleg
Or Alchochoden, Cuſpes, and Horroſcope.
Does not he cant? VVho here does vnderſtand him?

sp.speaker: ALM.
sp.l: This is no Canter, tho!

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: Or when my Maſter-Maſter
Talkes of his Tacticks, and his Rankes, and Files;
His Bringers vp, his Leaders on, and cries,
Faces about to the right hand, the left,
Now, as you were: then tells you of Redoubts,
Of Cats, and Cortines. Doth not he cant?

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Yes, 'faith.

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: My Eg-chind Laureat, here, when he comes forth
With Dimeters, and Trimeters, Tetrameters,

l.hi: Pentameters, Hexameters, Catalecticks,

sp.l: His Hyper, and his Brachy-Catalecticks,
His Pyrrhichs, Epitrites, and Choriambicks.
What is all this, but canting?

sp.speaker: MAD.
sp.l: A rare fellow!

sp.speaker: SHV.
sp.l: Some begging Scholler!

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: A decay'd Doctor at leaſt!

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Nay, I doe cheriſh vertue, though in rags.

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: And you, Mas Courtier.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Now he treats of you,
Stand forth to him, faire.

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: With all your fly-blowne proiects,
And lookes out of the politicks, your ſhut-faces,
And reſeru'd Queſtions, and Anſwers that you game with, As
Is't a Cleare buſineſſe? will it mannage well?
My name muſt not be vs'd elſe. Here, 'twill daſh.
Your buſineſſe has receiu'd a taint, giue off,
I may not proſtitute my ſelfe. Tut, tut,
That little duſt I can blow off, at pleaſure.
Here's no ſuch mountaine, yet, i'the whole worke!
But a light purſe may leuell. I will tyde
This affayre for you; giue it freight, and paſſage.
And ſuch mynt-phraſe, as 'tis the worſt of canting,
By how much it affects the ſenſe, it has not.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: This is ſome other then he ſeemes!

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: How like you him?

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: This cannot be a Canter!

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: But he is, Sir,
And ſhall be ſtill, and ſo ſhall you be too:
We'll all be Canters. Now, I thinke of it,
A noble Whimſie's come into my braine!

I'll build a Colledge,
Canters-Colledge, begun to be erected. I, and my Pecunia,
And call it Canters Colledge, ſounds it well?

sp.speaker: ALM.
sp.l: Excellent!

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: And here ſtands my Father Rector,
And you Profeſſors, you ſhall all profeſſe
Something, and liue there, with her Grace and me,
Your Founders: I'll endow't with lands, and meanes,
And Lickfinger ſhall be my Maſter-Cooke.
What? is he gone?

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: And a Profeſſor.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Yes.

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: And read Apicius de reculinaria
To your braue Doxi • , and you!

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: You, Couſin Fitton,
Shall (as a Courtier) read the politicks;
Doctor Al-manack, hee ſhall read Aſtrology,
Shunfield ſhall read the Military Arts.

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: That's Madrigall. As caruing, and aſſaulting the cold cuſtard.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: And Horace here, the Art of Poetry.
His Lyricks, and his Madrigalls, fine Songs,
Which we will haue at dinner, ſteept in claret,
And againſt ſupper, ſowc't in ſacke.

sp.speaker: MAD.
sp.l: In troth
A diuine Whimſey!

sp.speaker: SHV.
sp.l: And a worthy worke,
Fit for a Chronicle!

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Is't not?

sp.speaker: SHV.
sp.l: To all ages.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: And Pyed-mantle, ſhall giue vs all our Armes,
But Picklocke, what wouldſt thou be? Thou canſt cant too.

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: In all the languages in Weſtminſter-Hall,
Pleas, Bench, or Chancery. Fee-Farme, Fee-Tayle,
Tennant in dower, At will, For Terme of life,
By Copy of Court Roll, Knights ſeruice, Homage,
Fealty, Eſcuage, Soccage, or Frank almoigne,
Grand Sergeanty, or Burgage.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Thou appear'ſt,
〈 in non-Latin alphabet 〉 a Canter. Thou ſhalt read
All Littletons tenures to me, and indeed
All my Conueyances.

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: And make 'hem too, Sir?
Keepe all your Courts, be Steward o'your lands,
Let all your Leaſes, keepe your Euidences,
But firſt, I muſt procure, and paſſe your mort-maine
You muſt haue licence from aboue, Sir.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Feare not,
Pecunia's friends ſhall doe it.

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: But I ſhall ſtop it.
Your worſhips louing, and obedient father,
Your painefull Steward, and loſt Officer! Here his father diſcouers himſelfe.
Who haue done this, to try how you would vſe
Pecunia, when you had her: which ſince I ſee,
I will take home the Lady, to my charge,
And theſe her ſeruants, and leaue you my Cloak,
To trauell in to Beggers Buſh! A Seate,
Is built already, furniſh'd too, worth twentie
Of your imagin'd ſtructures, Canters Colledge.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: 'Tis his Father!

sp.speaker: MAD.
sp.l: Hee's aliue, me thinks.

sp.speaker: ALM.
sp.l: I knew he was no Rogue!

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: Thou, Prodigall,
Was I ſo carefull for thee, to procure,
And plot wi' my learn'd Counſell, Maſter Picklocke,
This noble match for thee, and doſt thou proſtitute,
Scatter thy Mistreſſe fauours, throw away
Her bounties, as they were red-burning coales,
Too hot for thee to handle, on ſuch raſcalls?
Who are the ſcumme, and excrements of men?
If thou had'ſt fought out good, and vertuous perſons
Of theſe profeſſions: I'had lou'd thee, and them,
For theſe ſhall neuer haue that plea 'gainſt me,
Or colour of aduantage, that I hate
Their callings, but their manners, and their vices.
A worthy Courtier, is the ornament
Of a Kings Palace, his great Maſters honour.
This is a moth, a raſcall, a Court-rat,
That gnawes the common-wealth with broking ſuits,
And eating grieuances! So, a true Souldier,
He is his Countryes ſtrength, his Soueraignes ſafety,
And to ſecure his peace, he makes himſelfe.
The heyre of danger, nay the ſubiect of it,
And runnes thoſe vertuous hazards, that this Scarre-crow
Cannot endure to heare of.

sp.speaker: SHV.
sp.l: You are pleaſant, Sir.

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: With you I dare be! Here is Pyed-mantle,
'Cauſe he's an Aſſe, doe not I loue a Herald?
Who is the pure preſeruer of deſcents,
The keeper faire of all Nobility,
Without which all would runne into confuſion?
Were he a learned Herald, I would tell him
He can giue Armes, and markes, he cannot honour,
No more then money can make Noble: It may
Giue place, and ranke, but it can giue no Vertue.
And he would thanke me, for this truth. This dog-Leach,
You ſtile him Doctor, 'cauſe he can compile
An Almanack; perhaps erect a Scheme
For my great Madams monkey: when 't has ta'ne
A gliſter, and bewrai'd the Ephemerides.
Doe I deſpiſe a learn'd Phyſician?
In calling him a Quack-Saluer? or blaſt
The euer-liuing ghirlond, alwaies greene
Of a good Poet? when I ſay his wreath
Is piec'd and patch'd of dirty witherd flowers?
Away, I am impatient of theſe vlcers,
(That I not call you worſe) There is no ſore,
Or Plague but you to infect the times. I abhorre
Your very ſcent. Come, Lady, ſince my Prodigall
Knew not to entertaine you to your worth,
I'll ſee if I haue learn'd, how to receiue you,
Hee points him to his patch'd cloake throwne off. With more reſpect to you, and your faire traine here.
Farewell my Begger in veluet, for to day,
To morrow you may put on that graue Robe,
And enter your great worke of Canters Colledge,
Your worke and worthy of a Chronicle,

div.head: The fourth Intermeane after the fourth Act.

sp.speaker: TATLE.
sp.p: Why? This was the worſt of all! the Cataſtrophe!

sp.speaker: CEN.
sp.p: The matter began to be good, but now: and he has ſpoyl'd it all, with his Begger there!

sp.speaker: MIRT.
sp.p: A beggerly Iacke it is, I warrant him, and a kin to the Poet.

sp.speaker: TAT.
sp.p: Like enough, for hee had the chiefeſt part in his play, if you marke it.

sp.speaker: EXP.
sp.p: Abſurdity on him, for a huge ouergrowne Play-maker! why ſhould he make him liue againe, when they, and we all thought him dead? If he had left him to his ragges, there had beene an end of him.

sp.speaker: TAT.
sp.p: I, but ſet a beggar on horſe-backe, hee'll neuer linne till hee be a gallop.

sp.speaker: CEN.
sp.p: The young heyre grew a fine Gentleman, in this laſt Act!

sp.speaker: EXP.
sp.p: So he did, Goſſip: and kept the beſt company.

sp.speaker: CEN.
sp.p: And feaſted 'hem, and his Miſtreſſe!

sp.speaker: TAT.
sp.p: And ſhew'd her to 'hem all! was not iealous!

sp.speaker: MIRTH.
sp.p: But very communicatiue, and liberall, and beganne to be magnificent, if the churle his father would haue let him alone.

sp.speaker: CEN.
sp.p: It was ſpitefully done o' the Poet, to make the Chuffe take him off in his heighth, when he was going to doe all his braue deedes!

sp.speaker: EXP.
sp.p: To found an Academy!

sp.speaker: TAT.
sp.p: Erect a Colledge!

sp.speaker: EXP.
sp.p: Plant his Profeſſors, and water his Lectures.

sp.speaker: MIRTH.
sp.p: With wine, goſſips, as he meant to doe, and then to defraud his purpoſes?

sp.speaker: EXP.
sp.p: Kill the hopes of ſo many towardly young ſpirits?

sp.speaker: TAT.
sp.p: As the Doctors?

sp.speaker: CEN.
sp.p: And the Courtiers! I proteſt, I was in loue with Maſter Fitton. He did weare all he had, from the hat-band, to the ſho • e-tye, ſo politically, and would ſtoop, and leere?

sp.speaker: MIRTH.
sp.p: And lie ſo, in waite for a piece of wit, like a Mouſe-trap?

sp.speaker: EXP.
sp.p: Indeed Goſſip, ſo would the little Doctor, all his behauiour was meere gliſter! O' my conſcience, hee would make any parties phyſicke i' the world worke, with his diſcourſe.

sp.speaker: MIR.
sp.p: I wonder they would ſuffer it, a fooliſh old fornicating Father, to rauiſh away his ſonnes Miſtreſſe.

sp.speaker: CEN.
sp.p: And all her women, at once, as hee did!

sp.speaker: TAT.
sp.p: I would ha' flyen in his gypſies face i' faith.

sp.speaker: MIRTH.
sp.p: It was a plaine piece of politicall inceſt, and worthy to be brought afore the high Commiſſion of wit. Suppoſe we were to cenſure him, you are the youngeſt voyce, Goſſip Tatle, beginne.

sp.speaker: TATLE.
sp.p: Mary, I would ha' the old conicatcher coozen'd of all he has, i'the young heyres defence, by his learn'd Counſell, Mr Picklocke!

sp.speaker: CENSVRE.
sp.p: I would rather the Courtier had found out ſome tricke to begge him, from his eſtate!

sp.speaker: EXP.
sp.p: Or the Captaine had courage enough to beat him.

sp.speaker: CEN.
sp.p: Or the fine Madrigall-man, in rime, to haue runne him out o' the Countrey, like an Iriſh rat.

sp.speaker: TAT.
sp.p: No, I would haue Maſter Pyed-mantle, her Graces Herald, to pluck downe his hatchments, reuerſe his coat-armour, and nullifie him for no Gentleman.

sp.speaker: EXP.
sp.p: Nay, then let Maſter Doctor diſſect him, haue him open'd, and his tripes tranſlated to Lickfinger, to make a probation diſh of.

sp.speaker: CEN. TAT.
sp.p: Agreed! Agreed!

sp.speaker: MIRTH.
sp.p: Faith I would haue him flat diſinherited, by a decree of Court, bound to make reſtitution of the Lady Pecunia, and the vſe of her body to his ſonne.

sp.speaker: EXP.
sp.p: And her traine, to the Gentlemen.

sp.speaker: CEN.
sp.p: And both the Poet, and himſelfe, to aske them all forgiueneſſe!

sp.speaker: TAT.
sp.p: And vs too.

sp.speaker: CEN.
sp.p: In two large ſheetes of paper—

sp.speaker: EXP.
sp.p: Or to ſtand in a skin of parchment, (which the Court pleaſe)

sp.speaker: CEN.
sp.p: And thoſe fill'd with newes!

sp.speaker: MIRTH.
sp.p: And dedicated to the ſuſtaining of the Staple!

sp.speaker: EXP.
sp.p: Which their Poet hath let fall, moſt abruptly?

sp.speaker: MIRTH.
sp.p: Banckruptly, indeede!

sp.speaker: CEN.
sp.p: You ſay wittily, Goſſip, and therefore let a proteſt goe out againſt him.

sp.speaker: MIR.
sp.p: A mourniuall of proteſts; or a gleeke at leaſt!

sp.speaker: EXP.
sp.p: In all our names▪

sp.speaker: CEN.
sp.p: For a decay'd wit—

sp.speaker: EXP.
sp.p: Broken—

sp.speaker: TAT.
sp.p: Non-ſoluent—

sp.speaker: CENSVRE.
sp.p: And, for euer, forfet—

sp.speaker: MIRTH.
sp.p: To ſcorne, of Mirth?

sp.speaker: TAT.
sp.p: Subſign'd. Tatle, Stay, they come againe.

div.head: ACT. V.
SCENE. I.
PENY-BOY. IV. to him THO. BARBER. after, PICKLOCKE.

sp.l: Hee comes out in the patchd cloak his father left him. NAy, they are fit, as they had been made for me,
And I am now a thing, worth looking at!
The ſame, I ſaid I would be in the morning.
No Rogue, at a Comitia of the Canters,
Did euer there become his Parents Robes
Better, then I do theſe: great foole! and begger!
Why doe not all that are of thoſe ſocieties,
Come forth, and gratulate mee one of theirs?
Me thinkes, I ſhould be, on euery ſide, ſaluted,
Dauphin of beggers! Prince of Prodigalls!
That haue ſo fall'n vnder the eares, and eyes,
And tongues of all, the fable o'the time,
Matter of ſcorne, and marke of reprehenſion!
I now begin to ſee my vanity,
Shine in this Glaſſe, reflected by the foile!
Where is my Faſhioner? my Feather-man?
My Linnener? Perfumer? Barber? all?
That tayle of Riot, follow'd me this morning?
Not one! but a darke ſolitude about mee,
Worthy my cloake, and patches; as I had
The epidemicall diſeaſe vpon mee:
And I'll ſit downe with it.

sp.speaker: THO.
sp.l: My Maſter! Maker!
How doe you? Why doe you ſit thus o'the ground, Sir?
Heare you the newes?

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: No, nor I care to heare none.
Would I could here ſit ſtill, and ſlip away
The other one and twenty, to haue this
Forgotten, and the day rac'd out, expung'd,
In euery Ephemerides, or Almanack.
Or if it muſt be in, that Time and Nature
Haue decree'd; ſtill, let it be a day
Of tickling Prodigalls, about the gills;
Deluding gaping heires, looſing their loues,
And their diſcretions; falling from the fauours
Of their beſt friends, and parents; their owne hopes;
And entring the ſociety of Canters.

sp.speaker: THO.
sp.l: A dolefull day it is, and diſmall times
Are come vpon vs: I am cleare vndone.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: How, Thom?

sp.speaker: THO.
sp.l: Why? broke! broke! wretchedly broke!

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Ha!

sp.speaker: THO.
sp.l: Our Staple is all to pieces, quite diſſolu'd!

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Ha!

sp.speaker: THO.
sp.l: Shiuer'd, as in an earth-quake! heard you not
The cracke and ruines? we are all blowne vp!
Soone as they heard th' Infanta was got from them,
Whom they had ſo deuoured i'their hopes,
To be their Patroneſſe, and ſoiourne with 'hem;
Our Emiſſaries, Regiſter, Examiner,
Flew into vapor: our graue Gouernour
Into a ſubt'let ayre; and is return'd
(As we doe heare) grand-Captaine of the Ieerers.
I, and my fellow melted into butter,
And ſpoyl'd our Inke, and ſo the Office vaniſh'd.
The laſt hum that it made, was, that your Father,
And Picklocke are fall'n out, the man o' Law. Hee ſtarts vp at thi • .

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: How? this awakes me from my lethargy.

sp.speaker: THO.
sp.l: And a great ſuite, is like to be betweene 'hem,
Picklocke denies the Feofement, and the Truſt,
(Your Father ſaies) he made of the whole eſtate,
Vnto him, as reſpecting his mortalitie,
When he firſt laid this late deuice, to try you.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Has Picklock then a truſt?

sp.speaker: THO.
sp.l: I cannot tell,
Here comes the worſhipfull—

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: What? my veluet-heyre, Picklocke enters.
Turn'd begger in minde, as robes?

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: You ſee what caſe,
Your, and my Fathers plots haue brought me to.

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: Your Fathers, you may ſay, indeed, not mine.
Hee's a hard hearted Gentleman! I am ſorie
To ſee his rigid reſolution!
That any man ſhould ſo put off affection,
And humane nature, to deſtroy his owne!
And triumph in a victory ſo cruell!
He's fall'n out with mee, for being yours,
And calls me Knaue, and Traytors to his Truſt,
Saies he will haue me throwne ouer the Barre—

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Ha'you deſeru'd it?

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: O, good heauen knowes
My conſcience, and the ſilly latitude of it!
A narrow minded man! my thoughts doe dwell
All in a Lane, or line indeed; No turning,
Nor ſcarce obliquitie in them. I ſtill looke
Right forward to th'intent, and ſcope of that
Which he would go from now.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Had you a Truſt, then?

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: Sir, I had ſomewhat, will keepe you ſtill Lord
Of all the eſtate, (if I be honeſt) as
I hope I ſhall. My tender ſcrupulous breſt
Will not permit me ſee the heyre defrauded,
And like an Alyen, thruſt out of the blood,
The Lawes forbid that I ſhould giue conſent,
To ſuch a ciuill ſlaughter of a Sonne.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Where is the deed? haſt thou it with thee?

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: No,
It is a thing of greater conſequence,
Then to be borne about in a blacke boxe,
Like a Low-countrey vorloffe, or Welſh-briefe.
It is at Lickfingers, vnder locke and key.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: O, fetch it hither.

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: I haue bid him bring it,
That you might ſee it.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Knowes he what brings?

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: No more then a Gardiners Aſſe, what roots he carries,

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: I was a ſending my Father, like an Aſſe,
A penitent Epiſtle, but I am glad
I did not, now.

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: Hang him, an auſtere grape,
That has no iuice, but what is veriuice in him.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Peny-boy runnes out to fetch his letter. I'll ſhew you my letter!

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: Shew me a defiance!
If I can now commit Father, and Sonne,
And make my profits out of both. Commence
A ſuite with the oldman, for his whole ſtate,
And goe to Law with the Sonnes credit, vndoe
Both, both with their owne money, it were a piece
Worthy my night-cap, and the Gowne I weare,
A Picklockes name in Law. Where are you Sir?
What doe you doe ſo long?

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: I cannot find
Where I haue laid it, but I haue laid it ſafe.

sp.speaker: PIC
sp.l: No matter, Sir, truſt you vnto my Truſt,
'Tis that that ſhall ſecure you, an abſolute deed!
And I confeſſe, it was in Truſt, for you,
Leſt any thing might haue hapned mortall to him:
But there muſt be a gratitude thought on,
And aid, Sir, for the charges of the ſuite,
Which will be great, 'gainſt ſuch a mighty man,
As is our Father, and a man poſſeſt
Of ſo much Land, Pecunia and her friends.
I am not able to wage Law with him,
Yet muſt maintaine the thing, as mine owne right,
Still for your good, and therefore muſt be bold
To vſe your credit for monies.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: What thou wilt,
So wee be ſafe, and the Truſt beare it.

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: Feare not,
'Tis hee muſt pay arrerages in the end.
Wee'l milke him, and Pecunia, draw their creame downe,
Before he get the deed into his hands.
My name is Picklocke, but hee'll finde me a Padlocke.

div.head: ACT. V.
SCENE. II.
PENY-BOY. CAN. PENY-BOY. IV. PICKLOCK. THO. BARBAR.

sp.l: HOw now? conferring wi'your learned Counſell,
Vpo' the Cheat? Are you o'the plot to coozen mee?

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: What plot?

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: Your Counſell knowes there, Mr Picklock,
Will you reſtore the Truſt yet?

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: Sir, take patience.
And memory vnto you, and bethinke you,
What Truſt? where doſt appeare? I haue your Deed,
Doth your Deed ſpecifie any Truſt? Is't not
A perfect Act? and abſolute in Law?
Seal'd and deliuer'd before witneſſes?
The day and date, emergent.

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: But what conference?
What othes, and vowes preceded?

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: I will tell you, Sir,
Since I am vrg'd of thoſe, as I remember,
You told me you had got a growen eſtate,
By griping meanes, ſiniſterly. (P. CA. How!)

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: And were
Eu'n weary of it; if the parties liued,
From whom you had wreſted it—(P. CA. Ha!)

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: You could be glad,
To part with all, for ſatisfaction:
But ſince they had yeelded to humanity,
And that iuſt heauen had ſent you, for a puniſhment
(You did acknowledge it) this riotous heyre,
That would bring all to beggery in the end,
And daily ſow'd conſumption, where he went—

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: You'old coozen both, then? your Confederate, too?

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: After a long, mature deliberation,
You could not thinke, where, better, how to place it—

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: Then on you, Raſcall?

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: What you pleaſe i'your paſſion,
But with your reaſon, you will come about
And thinke a faithfull, and a frugall friend
To be preferr'd.

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: Before a Sonne?

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: A Prodigall,
A tubbe without a bottome, as you term'd him;
For which, I might returne you a vow, or two,
And ſeale it with an oath of thankfulneſſe,
I not repent it, neither haue I cauſe, Yet—

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: Fore-head of ſteele, and mouth of braſſe! hath impudence
Poliſh'd ſo groſſe a lie, and dar'ſt thou vent it?
Engine, compos'd of all mixt mettalls! hence,
I will not change a ſyllab, with thee, more,
Till I may meet thee, at a Barre in Court,
Before thy Iudges.

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: Thither it muſt come,
His Son entreats him. Before I part with it, to you, or you, Sir.

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: I will not heare thee.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Sir, your eare to mee, though.
Not that I ſee through his perplexed plots,
And hidden ends, nor that my parts depend
Vpon the vnwinding this ſo knotted skeane,
Doe I beſeech your patience. Vnto mee
He hath confeſt the truſt.

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: How? I confeſſe it?

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: I thou, falſe man.

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: Stand vp to him, & confront him.

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: Where? when? to whom?

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: To me, euen now, and here,
Canſt thou deny it?

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: Can I eate, or drinke?
Sleepe, wake, or dreame? ariſe, ſit, goe, or ſtand?
Doe any thing that's naturall?

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Yes, lye:
It ſeemes thou canſt, and periure: that is naturall!

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: O me! what times are theſe! of frontleſſe carriage!
An Egge o'the ſame neſt! the Fathers Bird!
It runs in a blood, I ſee!

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: I'll ſtop your mouth.

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: With what?

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: With truth.

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: With noiſe, I muſt haue witnes.
Where is your witnes? you can produce witnes?

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: As if my teſtimony were not twenty,
Balanc'd with thine?

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: So ſay all Prodigalls,
Sicke of ſelfe-loue, but that's not Law, young Scatter-good.
I liue by Law.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Why? if thou haſt a conſcience,
That is a thouſsnd witneſſes.

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: No, Court,
Grants out a Writ of Summons, for the Conſcience,
That I know, nor Sub-paena, nor Attachment.
I muſt haue witneſſe, and of your producing,
Ere this can come to hearing, and it muſt
Be heard on oath, and witneſſe.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l:
Come forth, Hee produceth Thom.
Thom,
Speake what thou heard'ſt, the truth, and the whole truth,
And nothing but the truth. What ſaid this varlet?

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: A rat behind the hangings!

sp.speaker: THO.
sp.l: Sir, he ſaid
It was a Truſt! an Act, the which your Father
Had will to alter: but his tender breſt
Would not permit to ſee the heyre defrauded;
And like an alyen, thruſt out of the blood.
The Lawes forbid that he ſhould giue conſent
To ſuch a ciuill ſlaughter of a Sonne—

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: And talk'd of a gratuitie to be giuen,
And ayd vnto the charges of the ſuite;
Which he was to maintaine, in his owne name,
But for my vſe, he ſaid.

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: It is enough.

sp.speaker: THO.
sp.l: And he would milke Pecunia, and draw downe
Her creame, before you got the Truſt, againe.

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: Your eares are in my pocket, Knaue, goe ſhake 'hem,
The little while you haue them.

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: You doe truſt
To your great purſe.

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: I ha' you in a purſe-net,
Good Maſter Picklocke, wi'your worming braine,
And wrigling ingine-head of maintenance,
Which I ſhall ſee you hole with, very ſhortly.
A fine round head, when thoſe two lugs are off,
To trundle through a Pillory. You are ſure
You heard him ſpeake this?

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: I, and more.

sp.speaker: THO.
sp.l: Much more!

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: I'll proue yours maintenance, and combination,
And ſue you all.

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: Doe, doe, my gowned Vulture,
Crop in Reuerſion: I ſhall ſee you coyted
Ouer the Barre, as Barge-men doe their billets.

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: This 'tis, when men repent of their good deeds,
And would ha'hem in againe—They are almoſt mad!
But I forgiue their Lucida Interualla.

O, Lickfinger?
Pick-lock ſpies Lickfinger, and askes him aſide for the writing. come hither. Where's my writing?
I ſent it you, together with your keyes,

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: How?

sp.speaker: LIC.
sp.l: By the Porter, that came for it, from you,
And by the token, you had giu'n me the keyes,
And bad me bring it.

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: And why did you not?

sp.speaker: LIC.
sp.l: Why did you ſend a counter-mand?

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: Who, I?

sp.speaker: LIC.
sp.l: You, or ſome other you, you put in truſt.

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: In truſt?

sp.speaker: LIC.
sp.l: Your Truſt's another ſelfe, you know,
And without Truſt, and your Truſt, how ſhould he
Take notice of your keyes, or of my charge.

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: Know you the man?

sp.speaker: LIC.
sp.l: I know he was a Porter,
And a ſeal'd Porter for he bore the badge
On breſt, I am ſure.

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: I am loſt! a plot! I ſent it!

sp.speaker: LIC.
sp.l: Why! and I ſent it by the man you ſent
Whom elſe, I had not truſted.

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: Plague o'your truſt. Picklocke goes out.
I am truſs'd vp among you.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Or you may be.

sp.speaker: PIC.
sp.l: In mine owne halter, I haue made the Nooſe. Young Peny-boy diſcouers it, to his Father to be his plot of ſending for it by the Porter, and that hee is in poſſeſsion of the Deed.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: What was it, Lickfinger?

sp.speaker: LIC.
sp.l: A writing, Sir,
He ſent for't by a token, I was bringing it:
But that he ſent a Porter, and hee ſeem'd
A man of decent carriage.

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: 'Twas good fortune!
To cheat the Cheater, was no cheat, but iuſtice,
Put off your ragges, and be your ſelfe againe,
This Act of piety, and good affection,
Hath partly reconcil'd me to you.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: Sir.

sp.speaker: P.C.
sp.l: No vowes, no promiſes: too much proteſtation
Makes that ſuſpected oft, we would perſwade.

sp.speaker: LIC.
sp.l: Heare you the Newes? Elder Peny-boy ſtartles at the newes.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: The Office is downe, how ſhould we?

sp.speaker: LIC.
sp.l: But of your vncle?

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: No.

sp.speaker: LIC.
sp.l: He's runne mad, Sir.

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: How, Lickfinger?

sp.speaker: LIC.
sp.l: Stark ſtaring mad, your brother,
H'has almoſt kill'd his maid.

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: Now, heauen forbid.

sp.speaker: LIC.
sp.l: But that ſhe's Cat-liu'd, and Squirrill-limb'd,
With throwing bed-ſtaues at her: h'has ſet wide
His outer doores, and now keepes open houſe,
For all the paſſers by to ſee his iuſtice:
Firſt, he has apprehended his two dogges,
As being o'the plot to coozen him:
And there hee ſits like an old worme of the peace,
Wrap'd vp in furres at a ſquare table, ſcrewing,
Examining, and committing the poore curres,
To two old caſes of cloſe ſtooles, as priſons;
The one of which, he calls his Lollard's tower,
Th'other his Blocke-houſe, 'cauſe his two dogs names
Are Blocke, and Lollard.

sp.speaker: P. IV.
sp.l: This would be braue matter
Vnto the Ieerers.

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: I, If ſo the ſubiect
Were not ſo wretched.

sp.speaker: LIC.
sp.l: Sure, I met them all,
I thinke, vpon that queſt.

sp.speaker: P. CA.
sp.l: 'Faith, like enough:
The vicious ſtill are ſwift to ſhew their natures.
I'll thither too, but with another ayme,
If all ſucceed well, and my ſimples take.

div.head: ACT. V.
SCENE. IIIJ.
He is ſeene ſitting at his Table with papers before him. PENI-BOY. SEN. PORTER.

sp.l: WHere are the priſoners?

sp.speaker: POR.
sp.l: They are forth-comming, S•,
Or comming forth at leaſt.

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: The Rogue is drunke,
Since I committed them to his charge. Come hither,
Hee ſmells him. Neere me, yet neerer; breath vpon me. Wine!
Wine, o'my worſhip! ſacke! Canary ſacke!
Could not your Badge ha'bin drunke with fulſome Ale?
Or Beere? the Porters element? but ſacke!

sp.speaker: POR.
sp.l: I am not drunke, we had, Sir, but one pynt,
An honeſt carrier, and my ſelfe.

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: Who paid for't?

sp.speaker: POR.
sp.l: Sir, I did giue it him.

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: What? and ſpend ſixpence!
A Frocke ſpend ſixpence! ſixpence!

sp.speaker: POR.
sp.l: Once in a yeere, Sir,

sp.speaker: P. SE.
sp.l: In ſeuen yeers, varlet! Know'ſt thou what thou haſt done?
What a conſumption thou haſt made of a State?
It might pleaſe heauen, (a luſty Knaue and young)
To let thee liue ſome ſeuenty yeeres longer.
Till thou art foureſcore, and ten; perhaps, a hundred.
Say ſeuenty yeeres; how many times ſeuen in ſeuenty?
Why, ſeuen times ten, is ten times ſeuen, marke me,
I will demonſtrate to thee on my fingers,
Six-pence in ſeuen yeere (vſe vpon vſe)
Growes in that firſt ſeuen yeere, to be a twelue-pence.
That, in the next, two-ſhillings; the third foure-ſhillings;
